proposals.title: Fiscal Policy and Sectoral Transitions: The Macroeconomic Costs of Skill Mismatch and Retraining
proposals.motivation: The transition to a green, digital economy requires a massive reallocation of labor between sectors, a process complicated by worker skill heterogeneity. Pre-2026, standard macroeconomic models, including two-sector transition models, assume homogeneous labor, thereby ignoring the critical constraints of skill mismatch and the fiscal implications of active labor market policies. This omission risks leading to flawed policy advice on the speed of transition and the design of fiscal stabilizers.
proposals.hypothesis: Introducing skill heterogeneity and costly, time-consuming retraining into a two-sector transition model will significantly increase the estimated macroeconomic costs of rapid structural change and alter the optimal design of fiscal policy, particularly the role of automatic stabilizers and public investment in retraining.
proposals.method: We will extend a standard two-sector (e.g., carbon-intensive vs. green) New Keynesian DSGE model by introducing a continuum of worker skill types, following the incomplete markets literature. Sector-specific productivity will depend on skill type, and workers can invest in retraining to switch sectors, subject to a time and resource cost. The model will be calibrated to European economies using microdata on skill distributions (e.g., PIAAC) and retraining program effectiveness. We will solve the model using global solution methods to capture transition dynamics and perform counterfactual policy analysis on different fiscal rules, including Blanchard's proposed 'standards'.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a quantitative framework to assess the trade-offs between the speed of the green/digital transition and its short-to-medium-term employment and output costs. It will offer concrete, model-based guidance for redesigning EU fiscal rules to explicitly account for transition-related labor market frictions, thereby bridging the gap between macroeconomic stabilization and structural policy.
proposals.novelty: While skill heterogeneity is studied in labor economics, its integration into a macro-fiscal policy model of sectoral transition is novel. This moves beyond debates on aggregate deficits to analyze the composition of public spending (e.g., retraining subsidies vs. unemployment benefits) as a central element of fiscal design in a changing economy.

proposals.title: Fiscal Policy and External Imbalances: A Two-Country DSGE Model with Endogenous FDI and Export Dynamics
proposals.motivation: In the pre-2026 context of persistent global current account imbalances and rising protectionist pressures, understanding the interaction between domestic fiscal policy and international capital flows is critical. For European economies and others, fiscal deficits and public debt trajectories are increasingly influenced by foreign direct investment (FDI) and export performance, yet standard policy models often treat these as exogenous.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that in an open economy, the effectiveness of fiscal stabilization policy is significantly mediated by its impact on a country's attractiveness for FDI and its export competitiveness, with these channels potentially amplifying or dampening the domestic multiplier effects.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-country Dynamic Stochastic General Equilibrium (DSGE) model, extending standard New Keynesian frameworks used in Blanchard's prior work. The model will endogenize FDI decisions through a Tobin's q-style mechanism where multinational firms allocate capital based on expected returns, tax policies, and unit labor costs. Export demand will be modeled as a function of relative prices (real exchange rate) and foreign income, integrated with domestic consumption and investment decisions. The model will be calibrated to Eurozone and US data (pre-2025) and solved using perturbation methods (e.g., Dynare).
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a unified framework to analyze the joint determination of fiscal deficits, current accounts, and capital flows, offering clearer guidance for the design of EU fiscal rules in a globalized context. It will quantify how automatic stabilizers and discretionary fiscal policy transmit internationally, affecting external balances.
proposals.novelty: While two-country models exist, few integrate endogenous FDI with a detailed fiscal sector and export dynamics to explicitly study the feedback between fiscal policy design and external sustainability, a gap highlighted by debates on EU fiscal rule redesign.

proposals.title: Fiscal Institutions, External Constraints, and the Speed of Economic Transition: A Comparative Analysis of Central European Economies
proposals.motivation: The post-communist transition in Central Europe produced starkly different outcomes in growth, institutional quality, and convergence with the EU. Understanding the drivers of these variations is crucial for designing effective policies in other emerging economies and for the EU's own cohesion. This research addresses a core puzzle in development and European political economy by formally modeling the interplay of fiscal policy, external accounts, and institutional reform during this critical period.
proposals.hypothesis: The primary hypothesis is that the speed and success of economic transition were systematically determined by the initial strength of fiscal institutions and the interaction of fiscal policy with external constraints, rather than by reform sequencing or initial conditions alone.
proposals.method: The project will construct a panel dataset for Central European countries (e.g., Visegrád Group, Baltics, Balkans) from 1990-2015, integrating indicators of fiscal deficits, public debt, current account balances, and institutional quality. We will employ a dynamic panel model (System GMM) to account for endogeneity and persistence, testing how fiscal rules, automatic stabilizer design, and EU accession constraints interacted with market liberalization to shape transition paths. The analysis will be complemented by a comparative case study design to trace causal mechanisms in high- and low-speed transition countries.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a unified framework linking Blanchard's research on fiscal rules and automatic stabilizers to the political economy of transition. It will offer empirical evidence on how fiscal discipline, conditioned by external market pressures and European integration, catalyzed or hindered institutional development. The findings will contribute directly to contemporary debates on redesigning EU fiscal governance for heterogeneous member states.
proposals.novelty: While much literature focuses on macroeconomic stabilization or privatization, this proposal uniquely centers on the *fiscal-institutional nexus* as the engine of transition speed, formally modeling its interaction with the hard budget constraints imposed by global and European capital markets.

proposals.title: Labor Market Dynamics in a Low-Rate Era: A Longitudinal Test of the Blanchard Model
proposals.motivation: The post-Global Financial Crisis and post-COVID-19 periods have been characterized by persistently low natural rates of interest, challenging traditional macroeconomic policy frameworks. In this environment, the role of automatic stabilizers and the speed of labor market adjustment are critical for fiscal and monetary policy design, especially in European economies facing divergent recovery paths and fiscal constraints. Empirical validation of theoretical models predicting unemployment and transition dynamics is urgently needed to inform the redesign of EU fiscal rules and stabilization policy.
proposals.hypothesis: The central hypothesis is that the model's core prediction—that lower natural rates slow the post-shock adjustment speed of unemployment, increasing the efficacy of automatic stabilizers—holds empirically when tested against emerging longitudinal microdata from European countries.
proposals.method: The research will employ a multi-country longitudinal dataset, such as the EU-SILC panel, to track individual labor market transitions before and after macroeconomic shocks. We will implement a dynamic panel data model with country and time fixed effects, using system GMM estimators to address endogeneity. The key empirical specification will test the interaction between country-specific measures of the natural rate environment (proxied by pre-crisis safe real rates) and the responsiveness of unemployment duration to output gaps, controlling for national fiscal automatic stabilizer strength.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct empirical test of a leading theoretical model of labor markets in a low-rate environment, moving the debate from theoretical calibration to evidence-based assessment. It will yield concrete, quantified estimates of adjustment speeds crucial for calibrating the 'standards' proposed for next-generation EU fiscal frameworks and for assessing the relative potency of fiscal versus monetary stabilization tools.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in directly testing a high-level theoretical macroeconomic model with micro-level longitudinal data, bridging a gap between theory and granular empirics. Furthermore, it uniquely integrates the low-rate environment, a pre-2026 central puzzle, into an empirical analysis of labor market dynamics and fiscal policy design.

proposals.title: Optimal Time-Varying Unemployment Benefits: A Dynamic Model of Transitions and Stabilization
proposals.motivation: In the post-pandemic and low-interest-rate environment, the design of automatic stabilizers like unemployment insurance is central to fiscal policy. Traditional analyses treat benefit levels as static, ignoring how their optimal path should evolve during an individual's job search and the economy's recovery, a critical gap for designing effective European and national policies.
proposals.hypothesis: The optimal unemployment benefit profile is time-varying, initially high to support consumption and aggregate demand, then declining to incentivize job search, with its exact path contingent on the state of the business cycle and the speed of economic transitions.
proposals.method: We will develop a dynamic stochastic general equilibrium (DSGE) model with heterogeneous agents and search-and-matching frictions in the labor market, extending frameworks like Gertler and Trigari (2009). The model will incorporate a government sector with a detailed fiscal rule, allowing us to treat the benefit level and duration as control variables. We will use numerical optimal control methods (e.g., value function iteration and perturbation techniques) to solve for the welfare-maximizing time path of benefits under different shock scenarios (demand, supply, sectoral). Calibration will focus on European economies and the US, using pre-2026 OECD and Eurostat data.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a normative framework for designing 'smart' automatic stabilizers that dynamically adjust to economic conditions. It will offer concrete, model-based guidance for the redesign of fiscal rules and social insurance systems, contributing directly to debates on EU fiscal governance and stabilization policy in a low-rate era.
proposals.novelty: While the trade-off between insurance and incentives is well-known, formally modeling the *optimal dynamic path* of benefits during transition periods—integrating micro search incentives with macro stabilization goals—represents a significant advance over static or binary policy comparisons.

proposals.title: Technology Shocks and the Decoupling of Job Flows from Worker Reallocation: Implications for Fiscal Stabilization
proposals.motivation: Post-2008 and post-pandemic, advanced economies face persistent labor market frictions despite aggressive monetary and fiscal policy. Standard models assume job creation/destruction and worker flows are tightly coupled, but this may obscure how technology shocks (automation, digitalization) independently reshape labor demand. Understanding this decoupling is crucial for designing effective automatic stabilizers and EU fiscal rules that can address structural dislocations, not just cyclical unemployment.
proposals.hypothesis: Major technology and trade shocks drive gross job creation and destruction rates independently of the immediate reallocation decisions and mobility of workers, creating a 'reallocation gap' that varies across European countries and sectors.
proposals.method: We will employ a panel VAR framework on harmonized EU-LFS and OECD STAN data for European economies, decomposing job flow and worker flow series. To identify technology shocks, we will use a combination of industry-level TFP shocks and a novel instrumental variable based on global robot adoption trends (from IFR) and patent flows. A difference-in-differences design will compare job flow dynamics in high-exposure vs. low-exposure sectors following identified shocks, controlling for aggregate demand conditions using Blanchard-Quah restrictions.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide new empirical evidence on the independent drivers of job flows, challenging the standard search-and-matching paradigm. It will directly inform the debate on redesigning EU fiscal rules by quantifying the structural component of labor market turbulence that requires public investment, not just cyclical stabilization. The findings will refine models of automatic stabilizers in a low-rate environment by highlighting when traditional unemployment benefits may be insufficient.
proposals.novelty: While prior work links net employment to shocks, we test for the independence of gross job flows from concurrent worker flows—a stronger form of decoupling. Applying this lens to the institutional heterogeneity of European economies within a fiscal policy framework is a novel synthesis of labor macro and public finance.

proposals.title: Sectoral Heterogeneity in Job Flows: The Differential Impact of Employment Protection Legislation
proposals.motivation: The post-Global Financial Crisis and COVID-19 era has seen persistent debates on labor market flexibility and the design of fiscal stabilizers. Understanding how Employment Protection Legislation (EPL) affects job creation and destruction is crucial for designing effective fiscal and labor policies, especially in the European context where rules-based frameworks are being reconsidered. A sectoral analysis is vital because blanket labor market reforms may have asymmetric effects, potentially hindering reallocation in dynamic sectors while failing to protect jobs in vulnerable ones.
proposals.hypothesis: The dampening effect of strict EPL on gross job flows is not uniform across sectors; it is significantly stronger in sectors with higher intrinsic volatility (e.g., construction, retail) and lower in more stable or knowledge-intensive sectors (e.g., utilities, professional services).
proposals.method: We will employ a difference-in-differences methodology, leveraging reforms to EPL across European countries (OECD data) and sector-level job flow data from Eurostat and the OECD STAN database. The core specification will interact a country-level EPL strictness index (time-varying) with sector-level measures of exposure to demand shocks and technological change. We will use dynamic panel data models (System GMM) to control for endogeneity and include country-sector and year fixed effects. The analysis will separately model job creation and destruction rates as dependent variables.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide granular, evidence-based guidance for the redesign of EU fiscal and labor market rules, moving from one-size-fits-all standards to more nuanced, sector-sensitive policies. It will directly inform debates on automatic stabilizers by clarifying which sectors contribute most to labor market adjustment during downturns under different regulatory regimes.
proposals.novelty: While the aggregate relationship between EPL and job flows is studied, a systematic cross-country, cross-sector analysis linking EPL's heterogeneous effects to intrinsic sectoral characteristics remains underexplored. This approach integrates labor market dynamics with the structural analysis of economies, a perspective central to Blanchard's recent work on policy design.

proposals.title: Hazard Rates and Fiscal Buffers: A Survival Analysis of Unemployment Duration in European Economies
proposals.motivation: In the post-pandemic, low-rate environment, the effectiveness of automatic stabilizers and fiscal policy in mitigating labor market scarring is a central concern. Traditional unemployment models often fail to capture the dynamic, time-varying risk of long-term unemployment, which is crucial for designing effective EU fiscal standards and assessing current account pressures across member states. Understanding the precise hazard of exiting unemployment is key to evaluating the real-time impact of economic policies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the hazard rate of exiting unemployment exhibits significant non-proportionality, where the effects of key covariates—such as the size of automatic fiscal stabilizers, pre-existing current account positions, and country-specific labor market institutions—change over the duration of an unemployment spell.
proposals.method: We will apply semi-parametric Cox proportional hazards models to individual-level unemployment duration data from EU-SILC and national labor force surveys for a panel of European countries. To test for non-proportional hazards, we will use Schoenfeld residual tests and implement time-varying coefficient models or stratified Cox models. We will explicitly model the interaction of duration with macroeconomic variables (e.g., output gaps, fiscal deficits) and micro-level characteristics to estimate dynamic marginal effects.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a rigorous, dynamic framework to quantify how fiscal policy designs and macroeconomic conditions influence the risk of long-term unemployment over time. It will offer direct empirical evidence for the debate on redesigning EU fiscal rules by showing how different fiscal standards might affect labor market resilience, contributing to the literature on automatic stabilizers in a low-rate era.
proposals.novelty: While survival analysis is established in biostatistics, its application to model the hazard function of unemployment with a direct focus on testing the non-proportional effects of fiscal and macroeconomic policy variables is novel in macro-labor economics. This approach moves beyond static probit models to reveal how policy effectiveness decays or intensifies over an unemployment spell.

proposals.title: Vacancy Dynamics and Macroeconomic Stabilization: A Symmetric Analysis of Duration and Flows
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 macroeconomic policy, especially in the European context, has focused intensely on unemployment dynamics and fiscal deficits, with labor market analysis centered on job seekers. However, the post-pandemic recovery and persistent structural shifts have highlighted the critical role of job vacancies in determining employment outcomes and wage pressures. A symmetric, rigorous analysis of vacancy duration and creation/destruction flows is essential to understand matching efficiency, inflationary pressures, and the design of effective automatic stabilizers in a changing economy.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the dynamics of vacancy duration and vacancy flows are key, under-measured drivers of labor market tightness and macroeconomic volatility, with their behavior asymmetrically affected by fiscal and monetary policy shocks compared to unemployment dynamics.
proposals.method: The research will construct a novel panel dataset for European countries and the US, merging national vacancy surveys (e.g., JOLTS, Eurostat) with firm-level data where available. We will employ duration analysis (Cox proportional hazards models) to study vacancy survival, and adapt the stock-flow approach of labor market accounting to model vacancy creation and destruction rates. Vector Autoregression (VAR) models will be used to compare the impulse responses of vacancy and unemployment flows to identified policy and demand shocks, drawing on Blanchard's established methods for analyzing fiscal policy and market reactions.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first systematic, cross-country empirical analysis of vacancy dynamics as a symmetric counterpart to unemployment analysis. It will directly inform the redesign of EU fiscal rules by quantifying how labor demand shocks propagate, affecting automatic stabilizers. The findings will contribute to the debate on economic policy by offering a more complete framework for assessing labor market slack and inflationary risks.
proposals.novelty: While unemployment flows are well-studied, a symmetric, empirical treatment of vacancy duration and flows at a macroeconomic level is novel. Integrating this analysis into the core debate on fiscal rules and stabilization policy, rather than treating it as a micro-labor topic, represents a significant conceptual shift.

proposals.title: Heterogeneous Regional Labor Market Dynamics Under Uniform Fiscal Policy: A Flow-Based Analysis
proposals.motivation: In the post-2008 and post-COVID-19 era, a key policy puzzle is the persistent divergence in regional economic performance within integrated currency areas like the Eurozone, despite uniform monetary policy and national fiscal frameworks. Understanding the sub-national heterogeneity in labor market adjustment is crucial for designing effective automatic stabilizers and place-sensitive fiscal rules, themes central to Blanchard's recent work on EU fiscal redesign and stabilization in a low-rate environment.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that significant and persistent differences in labor market flow dynamics (job-finding and separation rates) and unemployment duration distributions exist across regions within a single country, and that these differences are systematically linked to local industrial composition and business cycle exposure rather than to institutional variation.
proposals.method: We will apply the labor market flow methodology (à la Shimer) to sub-national administrative data (e.g., from France's URSSAF or Italy's INPS) to construct consistent regional time series of job-finding and separation rates. We will employ duration analysis (survival models) to compare unemployment spells across regions. The core analysis will use panel regression and vector autoregression (VAR) techniques to decompose the drivers of regional differences and assess the sensitivity of regional labor markets to common national aggregate shocks, controlling for local sectoral structure.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a granular, flow-based map of regional labor market resilience and vulnerability within a national institutional setting. It will directly inform the debate on the design of EU fiscal standards by quantifying the need for regional stabilization mechanisms. It contributes to the literature on optimal currency areas by measuring adjustment margins below the national level.
proposals.novelty: While labor flow analysis is standard at the national level, its systematic application to sub-national regions within a country with uniform laws isolates the role of economic structure from institutions, a novel test of core labor market models. It bridges Blanchard's macro-policy focus with micro-founded labor search theory in a spatially disaggregated framework.

proposals.title: Monetary Policy in a Regime of Anchored Expectations: A Framework for Managing Large Residual Shocks
proposals.motivation: In the post-Global Financial Crisis and post-pandemic era, major advanced economies have largely succeeded in anchoring long-term inflation expectations, yet face heightened volatility from supply shocks, financial instability, and geopolitical events. Traditional monetary policy rules, which assume a stable Phillips curve and predictable transmission, are ill-equipped for this environment where large 'residual' shocks dominate business cycle fluctuations despite anchored expectations. This creates a critical gap in policy design for central banks, particularly in the European context where fiscal-monetary interactions are complex.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a monetary policy framework which explicitly decouples the response to expectation-anchoring shocks from the response to large residual volatility shocks will yield superior stabilization outcomes—lower output volatility and maintained credibility—compared to standard Taylor-type rules, especially when fiscal policy is constrained or pro-cyclical.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-pillar New Keynesian DSGE model, calibrated for the euro area and major advanced economies. The first pillar will model a strongly anchored expectations regime via a learning-algorithm that converges rapidly to the central bank target after small demand shocks. The second pillar will introduce a time-varying volatility process for residual shocks (e.g., energy prices, risk premia) estimated via GARCH models on pre-2026 data. Policy rules will be optimized using stochastic simulation and welfare-loss minimization, comparing a standard rule to a novel 'anchored-expectations residual targeting' (AERT) rule that adjusts the policy response function based on the identified source of the shock.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a formal, tractable framework for central banks to operate within a regime of credible inflation targeting while explicitly managing high residual volatility. It will offer concrete, optimized policy prescriptions for the European Central Bank and others, contributing directly to the debate on post-crisis monetary policy design. The work will also bridge the literature on monetary policy rules with that on fiscal stabilizers, assessing the necessary fiscal backstop for such a monetary framework.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in formally modeling monetary policy not for *achieving* anchored expectations, but for *operating optimally under the condition of already-anchored expectations* while facing large, non-inflationary shocks—a distinct post-2010s regime. This integrates Blanchard's recent focus on fiscal rules and stabilizers into the core monetary policy problem.

proposals.title: Modeling the Volatile Inflation-Unemployment Nexus: A Machine Learning Approach to Residual Variance
proposals.motivation: The breakdown of the stable Phillips curve relationship, particularly post-Global Financial Crisis, has posed a major challenge for monetary and fiscal policy. High residual volatility in inflation-unemployment models undermines the reliability of policy guidance, especially in a low-rate environment where automatic stabilizers and discretionary fiscal policy are crucial. Understanding the sources of this volatility is essential for redesigning effective macroeconomic frameworks, such as EU fiscal rules.
proposals.hypothesis: Machine learning techniques can identify and model the non-linear, high-dimensional drivers of residual variance in the inflation-unemployment relation, which are missed by traditional linear specifications. This variance is not purely random but is systematically linked to latent factors like sectoral shifts, global supply chain dynamics, and expectations formation.
proposals.method: We will employ a two-stage ML architecture on a panel dataset of advanced and emerging economies. First, we will use tree-based ensemble methods (Gradient Boosting, Random Forests) to perform variable selection and capture complex interactions among candidate predictors, including labor market flows, commodity prices, and financial conditions. Second, we will model the conditional variance of the residuals using a neural network-based heteroskedasticity model to directly learn the volatility structure as a function of economic state variables.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a more robust empirical foundation for policy analysis by quantifying the predictable component of inflation forecast errors. It will offer new insights for the debate on fiscal rules by showing how economic volatility interacts with automatic stabilizers. The methodology will advance the application of ML in macroeconomics beyond point prediction to the modeling of second moments.
proposals.novelty: While ML is increasingly used for macroeconomic forecasting, its application to explicitly model the *variance* of residuals in core structural relationships like the Phillips curve is novel. This moves beyond treating residuals as noise to learning their economic determinants, bridging ML techniques with fundamental macroeconomic policy concerns.

proposals.title: Financial Repression and Fiscal Space: A Comparative Analysis of Post-Communist and Developing Economies
proposals.motivation: In the post-2008 and post-COVID low-rate environment, fiscal policy space is constrained, reviving interest in financial repression as a tool for managing public debt. While the transition of post-Communist economies from repressed to market-based financial systems is well-documented, a systematic comparison with the persistent or evolving forms of repression in other developing regions (e.g., Latin America, Asia) is lacking. This gap limits our understanding of how different institutional legacies shape the fiscal-policy-financial-structure nexus.
proposals.hypothesis: The paper hypothesizes that the nature and fiscal consequences of financial repression in post-Communist economies were fundamentally distinct—more systemic but shorter-lived—than in other developing nations, leading to divergent long-term paths for fiscal sustainability and current account dynamics.
proposals.method: The research will employ a comparative panel data analysis for 40-50 countries from 1995-2025, grouping them into post-Communist, other developing, and advanced economies. We will construct a time-varying index of financial repression, synthesizing pre-2026 measures (e.g., directed lending, interest rate controls, capital account restrictions). This index will be used as a key regressor in fixed-effects models explaining fiscal deficits, public debt dynamics, and current account balances, controlling for standard macroeconomic variables. The analysis will specifically test for structural breaks and interaction effects with institutional quality indicators.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a unified empirical framework to compare financial repression across different developmental contexts, directly contributing to debates on fiscal rules and automatic stabilizers in emerging markets. It will offer concrete evidence on how historical policy choices constrain or enable fiscal space, informing the redesign of international financial architectures for developing economies.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in moving beyond the isolated study of transition economies to conduct a controlled, quantitative comparison with a broader set of developing nations, explicitly linking the legacy of repression to contemporary fiscal and external account challenges highlighted in Blanchard's recent work on EU fiscal rules and low-rate environments.

proposals.title: Financial Repression in Transition: Long-Run Effects on Fiscal Space, Capital Markets, and Political Institutions
proposals.motivation: The widespread use of financial repression—including directed lending, interest rate caps, and capital controls—by post-communist and other emerging economies in the 1990s and early 2000s was a critical, yet understudied, tool for managing fiscal deficits and stabilizing current accounts during the transition to market economies. Understanding its long-term consequences is vital for designing fiscal and financial policy frameworks in Europe and beyond, especially given the renewed debates on fiscal rules and the role of the state in economic stabilization.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that more intense early-transition financial repression had persistent negative effects on subsequent financial market depth and institutional quality, but may have created short-term fiscal space that allowed for smoother macroeconomic adjustment, with net long-run effects varying systematically across European and other emerging economies.
proposals.method: We will construct a novel cross-country panel dataset (1990-2025) measuring the intensity of financial repression (using indices based on policy variables like reserve requirements, state ownership in banking, and interest rate controls) for a sample of transition and emerging economies. We will employ a dynamic panel data approach (System GMM) and local projection methods to trace its multi-decade impact on outcomes including public debt trajectories, private credit depth, current account balances, and measures of institutional quality. The analysis will control for initial conditions, global financial cycles, and EU accession processes.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide the first systematic, long-horizon empirical assessment of financial repression as a transitional fiscal tool, directly contributing to modern debates on the redesign of EU fiscal rules and the trade-offs between short-term stabilization and long-term market development. It will offer concrete evidence on how past policy choices constrain current policy options in European and emerging economies.
proposals.novelty: While the short-term mechanics of financial repression are known, its enduring political-economic legacy remains a black box. This project uniquely scales the analysis across time to link 1990s policy to 2020s economic structures, integrating Blanchard's expertise in fiscal policy, transition, and European institutional design.

proposals.title: Wage Hysteresis, Dynamic Factor Demand, and the Design of Fiscal Stabilizers
proposals.motivation: Post-Global Financial Crisis and pandemic-era labor markets have shown persistent wage and employment effects, challenging standard models with static labor demand. For European economies and others facing low natural rates, understanding how firms' dynamic investment and hiring decisions interact with wage hysteresis is crucial for designing effective automatic stabilizers and sustainable fiscal rules.
proposals.hypothesis: Incorporating endogenous capital adjustment and dynamic labor demand into models with wage hysteresis will significantly alter the predicted long-run fiscal costs of recessions and the optimal calibration of fiscal policy rules.
proposals.method: We will develop and calibrate a multi-country DSGE model featuring heterogeneous firms. The core innovation is integrating capital adjustment costs and convex labor adjustment costs with a wage Phillips curve exhibiting strong hysteresis, as evidenced in post-2010 European data. The model will be solved using perturbation methods (Dynare) and estimated for a panel of EU countries using Bayesian techniques, explicitly testing the dynamic response of factor demand to persistent wage shocks.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a micro-founded framework to assess how hysteresis alters the trade-offs in EU fiscal rule design, moving from static compliance to dynamic standards. It will quantify the long-term budgetary impacts of recessions when capital and labor demand are jointly dynamic, offering concrete guidance for improving automatic stabilizers.
proposals.novelty: While hysteresis is often modeled on the wage/worker side, its feedback loop with firms' intertemporal investment and hiring decisions—a core channel for scarring—remains under-explored in policy-oriented macro-fiscal models.

proposals.title: Robustness and Identification in Monetary Union Entry: A Nonlinear Rational Expectations Approach to Euro Adoption Criteria
proposals.motivation: The debate on reforming EU fiscal rules and the persistent challenges of economic convergence within the Eurozone highlight a fundamental policy design problem. The standard Maastricht criteria are linear, backward-looking thresholds, but the underlying economies of candidate countries are complex, nonlinear systems. This creates a risk that ostensibly compliant paths could mask vulnerabilities that only become apparent post-entry, as seen in past accession episodes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that in a nonlinear rational expectations framework, observational equivalence emerges: multiple, theoretically distinct sets of entry criteria can generate identical observable macroeconomic trajectories for a candidate country, rendering the uniquely optimal policy non-identifiable from pre-entry data alone.
proposals.method: We will construct a nonlinear DSGE model for a small open economy aspiring to join a currency union, featuring financial frictions and endogenous risk premia. Using global solution methods (e.g., projection or perturbation to higher order), we will simulate the model under alternative candidate rule sets (e.g., different combinations of debt, deficit, and inflation targets). We will then employ a filtering and estimation exercise to test whether an econometrician could uniquely recover the true underlying rule from the simulated, nonlinear data, adapting identification tests from the SVAR literature to this policy context.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formal, model-based framework for assessing the robustness of monetary union entry criteria, moving beyond ad hoc adjustments to the Maastricht rules. It will contribute to the literature on policy design under uncertainty by highlighting identification limits as a key source of uncertainty, offering a new criterion—robustness to observational equivalence—for evaluating proposed entry standards.
proposals.novelty: While the observational equivalence problem is known in linear models, its implications in nonlinear settings for the design of *institutional entry rules* are unexplored. This fuses Blanchard's recent focus on fiscal rule redesign with a deeper methodological analysis of identification, a departure from purely linear or accounting-based assessments of convergence.

proposals.title: The Surplus Country's Dilemma: Strategic Delay and Credibility Under Asymmetric Information in a Monetary Union
proposals.motivation: Persistent current account surpluses within the Euro Area, particularly Germany's, have long been identified as a source of macroeconomic imbalance and a challenge for the ECB's single monetary policy. Pre-2026 policy debates focus on rules or political pressure to induce fiscal expansion in surplus countries. However, these frameworks ignore a critical real-world friction: national authorities possess superior, high-frequency information on domestic demand conditions compared to union-level institutions relying on lagged, aggregate data.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that this informational asymmetry creates a rational incentive for the surplus country to strategically delay agreed-upon fiscal adjustments, leading to prolonged intra-union imbalances and policy conflict, even when ex-ante commitments are made.
proposals.method: We will develop a dynamic game-theoretic model between a surplus country (with a private, real-time signal of domestic demand) and a common central bank/union authority. The model incorporates Bayesian updating, where the union must infer the true state from the country's observable actions (or inaction) and noisy public data. We will solve for Perfect Bayesian Equilibrium strategies, characterizing conditions for strategic delay. The model will be calibrated using pre-2026 Euro Area data (e.g., German fiscal plans, ECB forecasts, real-time data vintages from the ECB's Real-Time Database) to quantify the potential for and persistence of such delays.
proposals.contribution: This research provides a micro-founded explanation for the observed sluggish adjustment of surplus countries, moving beyond political economy arguments. It formally introduces asymmetric information as a source of policy conflict in a monetary union, offering a new lens to evaluate the credibility of fiscal rules and standards, such as those debated in the EU's fiscal framework review.
proposals.novelty: While the literature on fiscal policy coordination and current account rebalancing is vast, the explicit modeling of superior *real-time* national information as a driver of strategic delay in a surplus country's adjustment represents a novel contribution, distinct from standard analyses based on full information or political preferences alone.

proposals.title: Contingent Currency Regimes and Macroeconomic Stability: An Adaptive Learning Model for Portugal in the Euro Area
proposals.motivation: The post-crisis European policy debate, including the redesign of EU fiscal rules, has highlighted the tension between national fiscal needs and a common monetary policy. For a country like Portugal, with high public debt and persistent deficits, the credibility of its euro membership is a key determinant of financial conditions. Pre-2026, the theoretical literature largely treats currency union membership as an immutable state, failing to model how evolving market perceptions of its contingency can dynamically affect stability.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that when private agents use adaptive learning to form expectations about both within-regime variables and the probability of a regime shift (euro exit), the learning dynamics can generate endogenous instability, create hysteresis in inflation expectations, and amplify fiscal risk premia in a way that rational expectations models cannot capture.
proposals.method: We will develop a New Keynesian DSGE model for a small open economy (Portugal) with two policy regimes: the baseline euro regime and a counterfactual flexible exchange rate regime. Agents do not know the true transition probability between regimes. Instead, they estimate it and other model parameters in real-time using a constant-gain recursive least squares algorithm, updating their forecasts and policy functions accordingly. We will simulate the model to study how shocks to fiscal deficits or market sentiment interact with the learning process, potentially triggering non-linear jumps in sovereign spreads and inflation expectations.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formal framework to analyze 'exit premia' and the real-time credibility of fiscal consolidation within a monetary union. It will contribute to the debate on optimal fiscal rules by showing how their effectiveness depends on the learning dynamics of agents regarding regime sustainability. The model offers a new tool for assessing stability in European economies facing high debt.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the fusion of adaptive learning with a contingent currency regime model, moving beyond the standard rational expectations approach to euro membership. This allows for self-referential dynamics where beliefs about exit risk can become self-fulfilling, a channel absent in existing policy models.

proposals.title: News-Driven Fundamentals or Sunspot Equilibria? A Structural Empirical Analysis of Persistent Surpluses in the Eurozone Core
proposals.motivation: Persistent external surpluses and weak domestic demand in core Eurozone countries, notably Germany, present a major macroeconomic imbalance, constraining periphery adjustment and the union's overall stability. Pre-2026 policy debates, including on EU fiscal rule redesign, lack a micro-founded empirical account of whether these patterns reflect efficient forward-looking behavior or inefficient self-fulfilling expectations. Distinguishing between these explanations is crucial for determining if policy should accommodate structural trends or actively coordinate expectations to shift equilibria.
proposals.hypothesis: The observed macroeconomic dynamics of a surplus core country are more consistent with a sunspot-driven coordination on a low-demand, high-savings equilibrium than with a fundamentals-based explanation driven solely by news shocks about future productivity, demographics, or fiscal policy.
proposals.method: We will develop and estimate a two-country DSGE model for the Eurozone core (Germany) and periphery (aggregate), building on pre-2026 work by Farhi and Werning (2016) on fiscal unions and Bianchi and Ilut (2017) on confidence shocks. The model will feature a occasionally binding zero lower bound, incomplete risk-sharing, and a detailed fiscal sector. Using Bayesian methods (Metropolis-Hastings algorithm) with Eurozone data (1999-2024), we will estimate the relative contribution of news shocks (to TFP, survival rates, union risk) versus sunspot shocks, identified through sign restrictions derived from theory.
proposals.contribution: This research provides the first structural empirical test to disentangle news from sunspots in explaining Eurozone imbalances, moving beyond narrative accounts. It offers a micro-founded framework to assess whether surplus country behavior is a constraint requiring fiscal accommodation or an inefficiency requiring policy-led coordination, directly informing the ongoing debate on Eurozone fiscal architecture and stabilization mechanisms.
proposals.novelty: While sunspots and news shocks are studied separately, empirically distinguishing them in an open-economy union context is novel. The application to the core-periphery dynamic, rather than crisis episodes in the periphery, provides a new perspective on the Eurozone's stagnation-era challenges.

proposals.title: Disentangling Expectations and Rigidities: A Sufficient Statistics Approach for Fiscal and Monetary Policy Analysis
proposals.motivation: Post-crisis macroeconomic models increasingly incorporate lagged expectations to explain persistent deviations from rational forecasts, with significant implications for fiscal and monetary policy transmission. However, the empirical identification of this mechanism is confounded by alternative rigidities like sticky information or indexation. This creates an 'adversarial' identification problem in policy models, particularly relevant for analyzing automatic stabilizers and fiscal rules in European economies where policy lags and information sets are crucial.
proposals.hypothesis: A unique set of cross-equation restrictions and moment inequalities, derived from the dynamic structure of New Keynesian models, is necessary and sufficient to empirically identify lagged expectations formation against competing hypotheses using standard macroeconomic datasets.
proposals.method: We will derive the full set of admissible cross-equation restrictions for a canonical three-equation model with lagged expectations, following the methodology of Mavroeidis, Plagborg-Møller, and Stock (2014). The identification toolkit will employ Generalized Method of Moments (GMM) to test these restrictions against alternatives (e.g., rational inattention, indexation) using EU and US data on output, inflation, and interest rates. We will develop a two-step specification test: first, a J-test for over-identifying restrictions specific to the lagged expectations model; second, a non-nested test (e.g., Rivers-Vuong) to compare its fit against a menu of alternative models.
proposals.contribution: This research provides a practical, 'off-the-shelf' econometric toolkit for applied economists to validate a key structural assumption in policy models. It directly informs the design of fiscal rules and the assessment of stabilization policy by clarifying whether observed persistence stems from expectations or other rigidities, thereby grounding theoretical debates in empirical evidence.
proposals.novelty: While the theoretical possibility of lagged expectations is known, this work moves beyond discussion to provide the first operational set of *necessary and sufficient* conditions for its identification, translating a theoretical identification problem into a practical testing protocol usable with common data.

proposals.title: Expectations, Identification, and the Non-Tradable Productivity Channel: Implications for European Adjustment
proposals.motivation: Post-crisis European economies face persistent current account imbalances and low growth, with policy debates heavily focused on tradable sector competitiveness. However, the secular rise of the services sector suggests non-tradable productivity dynamics may be a crucial, under-analyzed driver of adjustment. Standard open-economy models, which rely on identifying restrictions tied to tradable shocks, may misattribute macroeconomic dynamics if agents form expectations based on lagged information about a non-tradable productivity transition, leading to potentially suboptimal fiscal and monetary policy.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that in economies with a large services sector, a sustained increase in non-tradable productivity, combined with agents forming expectations adaptively or with informational lags, can generate equilibrium dynamics (in current accounts, real exchange rates, and output) that are observationally equivalent to those driven by tradable-sector shocks, creating a fundamental identification problem for policymakers.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-sector (tradable/non-tradable) open-economy DSGE model for a representative European economy, incorporating a mechanism for lagged information or adaptive expectations formation regarding non-tradable productivity trends. Using Bayesian estimation techniques (e.g., Metropolis-Hastings algorithm) on euro area data (pre-2025), we will test the model's identification by assessing whether the posterior distributions for key structural parameters (e.g., the relative volatility and persistence of sectoral productivity shocks) are well-determined. We will conduct a series of counterfactual simulations to isolate the macroeconomic implications of the non-tradable productivity channel under different expectation formation regimes.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a novel framework for interpreting European macroeconomic adjustment, challenging the primacy of tradable-sector explanations. It will offer concrete methodological insights into identification pitfalls in multi-sector models with informational frictions. The findings will directly inform the design of more robust EU fiscal rules and stabilization policies by clarifying the underlying drivers of deficits and imbalances.
proposals.novelty: While sectoral models exist, the explicit fusion of a non-tradable productivity transition with lagged expectation formation to create an identification problem is a new angle. It moves beyond the standard 'competitiveness' narrative dominant in European policy circles, applying insights from information frictions to a critical, real-world policy dilemma.

proposals.title: Fundamentalness Tests and Causal Inversion: Adjudicating Boom-Bust Narratives in European Periphery Economies
proposals.motivation: Post-euro entry booms and subsequent slumps in European periphery countries, such as Spain and Ireland, have competing causal explanations. One narrative posits self-fulfilling optimism as a non-fundamental 'sunspot' shock, while another argues pre-existing structural weaknesses and fiscal trends were the fundamental drivers. Distinguishing between these is crucial for designing effective fiscal and monetary policy frameworks for the EU, yet existing methods rely on narrative identification without formal tests of the underlying informational assumptions.
proposals.hypothesis: The shock series implied by the 'sunspot' narrative of self-fulfilling optimism will be found to be non-fundamental for the observed data, while the shock series implied by the narrative of pre-existing structural and fiscal deficits will be fundamental, thereby inverting the presumed direction of causality in standard boom-bust accounts.
proposals.method: We will estimate a Structural Vector Autoregressive (SVAR) model for a selected Euro-area periphery economy using pre-2025 data on key variables (e.g., credit spreads, investment, consumption, current account, fiscal balance). We will employ the statistical toolkit for non-fundamentalness (e.g., tests based on the rank of spectral density matrices, as in Forni et al., 2019; the information criterion approach of Lippi & Reichlin, 1994; and the direct test of fundamentalness in Chen et al., 2011) on the SVAR's moving average representation. The core test will determine whether the shock series recovered under each competing narrative identification scheme is fundamental (i.e., in the space spanned by present and past observations) or not.
proposals.contribution: This research provides a formal econometric framework to test the causal structure of competing economic narratives, moving beyond informal storytelling. It directly contributes to the redesign of EU fiscal rules by offering evidence on whether booms are driven by exogenous sentiment or endogenous fiscal and structural factors. The method bridges advanced time-series econometrics with substantive policy debates on automatic stabilizers and the origins of macroeconomic crises.
proposals.novelty: While tests for non-fundamentalness exist, their application is novel in this context: we synthesize them with explicit narrative identifications to perform a causal inversion test, directly adjudicating between sunspot and fundamental explanations of a major real-world policy episode.

proposals.title: Fiscal Rules, Nonlinearities, and Observational Equivalence: An Empirical DSGE Analysis for European Economies
proposals.motivation: The redesign of EU fiscal rules and the debate on fiscal policy in a low-rate environment highlight the critical need for reliable empirical models to guide policy. However, the theoretical problem of observational equivalence—where different structural models or parameter sets generate similar observable dynamics—poses a fundamental challenge to such empirical guidance. This issue is particularly acute for medium-scale DSGE models used by central banks and fiscal authorities, as it can obscure whether observed outcomes stem from policy rule changes, shifts in shock processes, or model nonlinearities.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the nonlinear structure of a medium-scale DSGE model, when estimated with advanced Bayesian techniques, will reveal empirically significant regions of parameter space where observational equivalence between fiscal policy rules and shock processes is quantitatively material, thereby complicating counterfactual policy analysis.
proposals.method: We will develop a medium-scale nonlinear DSGE model for a representative European economy, incorporating financial frictions, a detailed fiscal bloc, and a zero lower bound constraint. The model will be estimated using full-information Bayesian methods (Metropolis-Hastings and Sequential Monte Carlo algorithms) on pre-2025 Eurozone data. To quantify observational equivalence, we will employ Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) diagnostics, posterior predictive checks, and a novel application of the Kullback-Leibler divergence to compare the predictive densities of models under alternative fiscal rule and shock process specifications.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide the first empirical quantification of the practical identifiability challenges posed by observational equivalence in a policy-relevant, nonlinear DSGE framework. It will directly inform ongoing debates on fiscal rule design by clarifying the limits of inference from aggregate data. The findings will bridge a critical gap between abstract theoretical equivalence results and the concrete models used for macroeconomic forecasting and policy simulation.
proposals.novelty: While observational equivalence is a known theoretical concept, its empirical magnitude in modern, nonlinear, estimated DSGE models remains unexplored. This project uniquely fuses the estimation challenges of medium-scale models with a direct test of a core identification problem, moving beyond linear approximations and small-scale theoretical examples.

proposals.title: News Shocks and Regional Divergence: An Inverse Observational Equivalence Approach to Portuguese Adjustment
proposals.motivation: Within the European Monetary Union, persistent regional imbalances pose a major challenge to stabilization policy. Pre-2026 debates on redesigning EU fiscal rules and the role of automatic stabilizers in a low-rate environment highlight the need to understand the drivers of regional divergence, particularly anticipatory dynamics. Portugal, with its distinct regional economies like the tourism-dependent Algarve or industrial Norte, offers a critical case to study how expectations of future national or EU policy shape current account and unemployment dynamics.
proposals.hypothesis: The paper hypothesizes that a significant portion of observed regional adjustment patterns in Portugal can be uniquely attributed to structural news shocks about future fiscal or integration policies, rather than to contemporaneous fundamental shocks or non-fundamental sunspots.
proposals.method: We treat a Portuguese region (e.g., Algarve) as a small open economy within a national framework. We first estimate a structural vector autoregression (SVAR) on regional data (current account, unemployment, sectoral composition, investment). We then apply the inverse observational equivalence mapping, as developed in pre-2026 literature, by imposing institutional and structural details of the region (e.g., reliance on EU funds, sectoral rigidity, labor mobility) to discipline the identification. This involves solving for the set of news shock processes (about future TFP, demand, or policy) that, within a calibrated DSGE model of a regional small open economy, generate impulse response functions that match the estimated SVAR patterns.
proposals.contribution: This research provides a novel empirical method to identify the role of anticipatory dynamics in regional economic divergence. It contributes to the literature on fiscal policy design and automatic stabilizers by quantifying how expectations of future policy drive current imbalances, offering insights for place-based stabilization tools within a currency union.
proposals.novelty: The application of the inverse observational equivalence framework to a sub-national context is novel, using region-specific institutional details to solve the identification problem for news shocks that is typically indeterminate at the national level.

proposals.title: The Great Moderation as a Regime Shift: Did Reduced Macroeconomic Volatility Cause the Decline in the Natural Rate of Interest?
proposals.motivation: The post-1980s Great Moderation, characterized by lower macroeconomic volatility, is widely attributed to improved monetary policy. However, this narrative assumes policy as the primary cause. In the pre-2026 context of persistently low natural rates (r*) and debates on fiscal-monetary policy space, it is crucial to test the reverse causality: whether the exogenous component of the Great Moderation itself altered the economic environment, leading to a lower r* and thus constraining central bank policy options.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a significant portion of the observed decline in the global natural rate of interest (r*) since the 1980s was not an independent shock but an endogenous outcome of the reduction in macroeconomic volatility associated with the Great Moderation, which reduced precautionary savings motives and increased the demand for safe assets.
proposals.method: We will employ a two-stage empirical strategy focusing on advanced economies, particularly the US and European countries. First, we will use a time-varying parameter vector autoregression (TVP-VAR) with stochastic volatility to identify exogenous shocks to macroeconomic volatility (e.g., in output and inflation). Second, we will use local projections to trace the dynamic response of proxies for r* (e.g., filtered real rates, Laubach-Williams estimates) to these identified volatility shocks. We will control for standard r* drivers (trend growth, demographics) to isolate the volatility channel.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a novel, empirically grounded explanation for the secular decline in r*, challenging the standard 'exogenous shift' narrative. It will contribute to the redesign of fiscal and monetary policy frameworks by clarifying the historical interdependence of volatility and the policy space, directly informing Blanchard's work on EU fiscal rules and stabilization in a low-rate environment.
proposals.novelty: The analysis inverts the standard causal direction in the literature on the Great Moderation and r*, treating reduced volatility as a cause rather than a consequence of policy. It formally tests this inverted relationship using state-of-the-art pre-2026 identification techniques from the volatility literature.

proposals.title: Monetary Policy and the Persistence of Aggregate Demand: A Structural Analysis of Interest Rates and Sales Autocorrelation
proposals.motivation: The post-Global Financial Crisis period, characterized by persistently low interest rates and unconventional monetary policy, has coincided with a potential shift in macroeconomic dynamics, including the behavior of business cycles. Pre-2026 research, including Blanchard's work on low-rate environments, suggests that the traditional persistence of economic variables like sales may be weakening. Understanding this link is crucial for designing effective fiscal and monetary policy, especially in the European context where fiscal rules are being reconsidered and the efficacy of automatic stabilizers is in question.
proposals.hypothesis: The conduct of monetary policy, particularly the level and volatility of the policy interest rate, structurally influences the degree of autocorrelation in aggregate sales and output. Specifically, a lower and more stable interest rate environment reduces the persistence of demand shocks, leading to less autocorrelated sales growth.
proposals.method: We will develop a New Keynesian Dynamic Stochastic General Equilibrium (DSGE) model, augmented with financial frictions and a detailed transmission mechanism from policy rates to firm-level pricing and inventory decisions. The model will be estimated using Bayesian methods on pre-2026 panel data for European countries and other advanced economies, explicitly testing for a time-varying parameter capturing sales persistence. The core test will involve modeling the persistence parameter as a function of the monetary policy regime's characteristics (e.g., average rate, volatility, ZLB duration), using a Markov-Switching or a smooth transition framework to identify structural breaks.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a formal, structural account of how monetary policy regimes shape business cycle dynamics, moving beyond reduced-form correlations. It will offer quantitative evidence to inform the debate on redesigning EU fiscal rules by clarifying the interaction between monetary policy and the inherent stability of the economy. The findings will also contribute to understanding the limitations of automatic stabilizers in a new macroeconomic environment.
proposals.novelty: While reduced-form evidence on changing macroeconomic persistence exists, this proposal formally models the *causal channel* from the interest rate process to sales autocorrelation within a structural framework, integrating monetary policy design directly into the persistence-generating mechanism of the economy.

proposals.title: Fiscal Architecture for Symmetric Adjustment: A Blueprint for a Viable Wage-Led Strategy in the Eurozone
proposals.motivation: The Eurozone's post-crisis adjustment relied heavily on internal devaluation in deficit countries, a process with severe social and political costs. While demand-side or coordinated wage-led strategies are proposed as alternatives, they are deemed non-viable under the union's existing institutional architecture, particularly its incomplete fiscal and banking unions and restrictive fiscal rules. This research addresses the critical gap between theoretical policy alternatives and their practical feasibility, asking what must structurally change to make them work.
proposals.hypothesis: A wage-led, symmetric adjustment strategy within a monetary union is stable only under a specific set of institutional preconditions, including a central fiscal capacity with significant automatic stabilizers, a unified banking union with common deposit insurance, and reformed fiscal rules that act as standards for sustainable demand management rather than rigid numerical constraints.
proposals.method: The methodology combines comparative political economy analysis of historical monetary unions (e.g., the US, Germany pre-1990) with formal modeling. We will build and calibrate a two-country New Keynesian DSGE model of a currency union, explicitly incorporating a central fiscal authority and a banking sector. The model will be used to run counterfactual simulations, testing the stability of wage-led adjustment paths under varying institutional configurations (e.g., different sizes of a European unemployment reinsurance scheme, different designs of the SGP's 'standards').
proposals.contribution: This work moves the debate from identifying policy alternatives to specifying the necessary institutional reforms, providing a concrete 'roadmap' for structural change in the Eurozone. It formally demonstrates the complementarities between fiscal capacity, banking union, and flexible rules, offering a coherent package for policymakers seeking to escape the internal devaluation imperative.
proposals.novelty: It treats the union's architecture as the primary endogenous variable to be redesigned, rather than a fixed constraint, and integrates the analysis of fiscal rules as 'standards' with the operational requirements of a central fiscal stabilisation function.

proposals.title: From Rules to Rupture: A Bayesian Early-Warning Framework for Conditional Euro-Area Exit Scenarios in Portugal
proposals.motivation: The post-2010 European sovereign debt crisis revealed the fragility of incomplete monetary unions, yet the policy and academic discourse on euro-area exit remains binary and often speculative. For a country like Portugal, with high public debt and vulnerability to external shocks, the credibility of fiscal and macroeconomic policy depends on understanding the precise conditions under which euro membership could become unsustainable. This research addresses a critical gap in the design of fiscal rules and stabilization policy by moving from abstract exit narratives to a quantifiable, conditional risk assessment framework.
proposals.hypothesis: A Bayesian early-warning model, trained on historical episodes of monetary union stress, can identify a specific, multi-dimensional threshold of economic and political indicators beyond which a Portuguese euro exit transitions from a tail risk to a non-negligible policy contingency.
proposals.method: Stage 1 develops a Bayesian Logistic Regression or Markov-Switching model, using data from past currency union dissolutions (e.g., USSR, Czechoslovakia) and severe EMU stress episodes (2010-2015). The model is calibrated on indicators such as Target2 imbalances, sovereign-bank doom loop intensity, political fragmentation indices, and primary deficit trajectories. Stage 2 employs this model as a trigger mechanism within a multi-country DSGE model for the euro area; exit scenarios (involving new currency issuance and debt redenomination) are only simulated when projected or observed data crosses the probabilistic threat thresholds identified in Stage 1.
proposals.contribution: It provides a rigorous, model-based tool for stress-testing EU fiscal rules and national stabilization policies against a credible, conditional exit risk. The output is a set of actionable early-warning indicators and conditional economic pathways, moving the debate beyond speculation to a structured analysis of crisis mechanics and policy failure points.
proposals.novelty: It innovates by treating euro exit not as an exogenous assumption but as an endogenous, probability-weighted outcome triggered by specific, measurable deteriorations in fundamentals, directly linking the literature on fiscal rules and automatic stabilizers to an adversarial risk analysis of monetary union integrity.

proposals.title: Narrative Asymmetry in the Eurozone Boom: A Computational Text Analysis of Pre-Crisis Risk Awareness
proposals.motivation: Post-crisis narratives often attribute the Eurozone crisis to pre-existing structural weaknesses in periphery economies. However, this risks ex-post rationalization. Understanding the actual political and policy discourse *during* the pre-2008 boom is crucial for evaluating the efficiency of policy frameworks and the role of market sentiment. It directly informs debates on fiscal rules, automatic stabilizers, and the design of institutions to mitigate boom-bust cycles in a monetary union.
proposals.hypothesis: The political and policy salience of structural weakness narratives (e.g., low productivity growth, labor market rigidities) was systematically low during the pre-crisis boom in Eurozone periphery countries, being overwhelmingly dominated by optimistic narratives of convergence and euro benefits, which supports the inverted causality hypothesis where the bust revealed rather than caused the focus on these weaknesses.
proposals.method: We will construct a diachronic corpus (2002-2007) of text from major newspapers, parliamentary debates, and central bank publications for Greece, Ireland, Italy, Portugal, and Spain. Using pre-trained transformer models like BERT, we will perform topic modeling (BERTopic) and fine-tune classifiers to quantify the relative frequency and sentiment of documents discussing pre-defined structural weakness themes versus euro-optimism themes. We will track the co-evolution and network of these discourses over time and across institutional sources.
proposals.contribution: This study provides the first large-scale, data-driven map of pre-crisis policy awareness, moving beyond theoretical claims to quantify the 'narrative asymmetry' that preceded the crisis. It offers empirical evidence for macroeconomic models incorporating narrative-driven cycles and contributes concrete metrics for evaluating the early warning function of political and media discourse.
proposals.novelty: While political salience is often discussed qualitatively, this project operationalizes it quantitatively for a key historical period using state-of-the-art NLP on a multi-source corpus. It directly tests a causal historical claim about the Eurozone crisis with a novel methodological synergy from computational social science.

proposals.title: Regime Shifts and Resilience in the Eurozone Periphery: A Comparative Analysis of Adjustment Pathways in Portugal, Ireland, Greece, and Spain
proposals.motivation: The divergent post-crisis trajectories of Eurozone peripheral economies, despite facing similar external shocks and monetary constraints, challenge standard macroeconomic convergence models. Understanding the factors that determined these pathways—rapid recovery versus prolonged depression—is crucial for designing effective EU fiscal and structural policy frameworks, especially in a low-rate environment where automatic stabilizers and fiscal space are constrained. This analysis is vital for preventing future asymmetric crises within the monetary union.
proposals.hypothesis: The divergent recovery pathways of Portugal, Ireland, Greece, and Spain can be systematically explained by differences in pre-crisis institutional and economic 'resilience buffers,' which determined each economy's vulnerability to a regime-shifting shock and its capacity for post-shock reorganization.
proposals.method: The research employs a comparative case study design, treating each national economy as a complex system. We will construct a panel dataset of key 'state variables' (e.g., current account balances, unit labor costs, private/public debt, banking sector leverage, labor market indices) from euro entry through 2019. Using methods from empirical macroeconomics and comparative political economy, we will identify pre-crisis stability basins, the crisis tipping point, and post-crisis trajectories. The analysis will involve regime shift detection algorithms (e.g., sequential t-tests, Markov-switching models) and qualitative comparative analysis (QCA) to isolate the necessary and sufficient institutional conditions for different recovery classes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, systematic framework for classifying economic crises and recoveries within a monetary union, moving beyond narrative accounts. It will generate specific, testable insights into which institutional configurations (e.g., in labor markets, banking supervision, fiscal governance) act as critical resilience factors, directly informing the ongoing debate on redesigning EU fiscal rules and crisis management frameworks.
proposals.novelty: The explicit adaptation of ecological resilience and regime shift theory to comparative macroeconomics represents a significant conceptual innovation, offering a dynamic, systems-based alternative to standard equilibrium models for analyzing crisis episodes in the Eurozone periphery.

proposals.title: Quantifying Crisis Regimes: A Machine Learning Approach to Model Discrimination in Eurozone Periphery Adjustments
proposals.motivation: The post-2008 adjustment paths of Eurozone periphery countries exhibited stark differences in sovereign spreads, growth, and unemployment, fueling debates on whether crises were driven by fundamentals or self-fulfilling expectations. Resolving this is critical for designing effective EU fiscal and financial stability policy, as the appropriate response depends on the underlying crisis mechanism. Current empirical work relies on narrative classifications or reduced-form tests, lacking a unified framework for rigorous model comparison.
proposals.hypothesis: The adjustment trajectories in Portugal, Ireland, Greece, and Spain during critical periods (e.g., 2010-2015) are best explained by distinct theoretical regimes, with some countries' dynamics aligning with fundamental 'news' models and others with multiple-equilibria 'sunspot' models.
proposals.method: We will simulate synthetic time-series data for key variables (sovereign spreads, GDP growth, unemployment) from competing calibrated DSGE models representing 'news' and 'sunspot' theories. Using these simulated datasets as labeled training data, we will train supervised classifiers—specifically Random Forests for interpretability and Gradient Boosted Trees for predictive power—to distinguish between theoretical regimes. The trained classifiers will then be applied to actual empirical time-series from each peripheral country to generate probabilistic classifications of the dominant regime in each episode.
proposals.contribution: This provides a novel, quantitative basis for comparing adjustment regimes across countries, moving beyond narrative case studies. It directly tests the empirical relevance of competing crisis theories in a unified framework, offering evidence-based guidance for the redesign of EU fiscal rules and crisis management tools tailored to different types of macroeconomic instability.
proposals.novelty: Applying machine learning for model discrimination to this macroeconomic question is novel; it automates and objectifies the classification of crisis episodes, which has traditionally been a theoretical and narrative exercise. The fusion of simulated data from structural models with empirical classification algorithms offers a new methodological bridge between theory and data in international macroeconomics.

proposals.title: Robustness and Multiplicity: A Nonlinear Expectations Analysis of Structural Reforms in Portugal's Tradable Sector
proposals.motivation: Post-crisis, the effectiveness of structural reforms in European economies like Portugal remains debated, especially in a low-rate environment where fiscal space is constrained. Standard linear models may fail to capture reform impacts during crises, where shocks are non-Gaussian and confidence-driven equilibria shifts are plausible. This research addresses a core EU policy dilemma: identifying which reforms yield robust productivity gains under extreme conditions versus those whose benefits depend critically on favorable expectations.
proposals.hypothesis: The estimated productivity impact of structural reforms on Portugal's tradable sector is not invariant to shock distributions and expectation formation; specifically, under non-Gaussian shocks and endogenous confidence, the observational equivalence between different reform types breaks down, revealing distinct robustness profiles and potential for multiple equilibria.
proposals.method: We will develop a nonlinear rational expectations model for Portugal's tradable sector, incorporating strategic complementarities that can generate multiple equilibria. Using firm- and sector-level data, we will estimate the model via Simulated Method of Moments (SMM), explicitly comparing Gaussian versus fat-tailed (e.g., Student's t) and skewed shock distributions. We will employ global solution methods (e.g., time iteration on a grid) to capture nonlinear dynamics and use stochastic simulations to map reform parameters to equilibrium distributions of productivity outcomes, testing for bifurcations.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, empirically grounded framework for assessing the robustness of structural reforms to extreme events, directly informing the redesign of EU fiscal and structural governance. It will quantify the role of confidence channels in reform success, offering a micro-founded perspective on when and why reforms can trigger virtuous cycles or stall.
proposals.novelty: It integrates the analysis of non-Gaussian shocks and multiple equilibria into the empirical evaluation of structural reforms, moving beyond linear treatment effects. This fusion tests whether the policy prescriptions from standard models are fundamentally altered by the nonlinearities prevalent in real-world crises.

proposals.title: Fiscal Space and Structural Reform: A Quantitative Assessment of Productivity Gains in Portugal's Tradable Sector
proposals.motivation: In the post-2010 European sovereign debt crisis context, Portugal, like other Southern European economies, faces persistent low productivity growth and large public debt, constraining fiscal policy. Structural reforms are widely advocated to boost potential output, but their quantified impact on the tradable sector—critical for external sustainability—remains debated, complicating the calibration of EU fiscal rules and the design of credible medium-term fiscal consolidation plans.
