ideas.title: Extend analysis to more countries beyond US and France
ideas.description: Apply the same structural shock identification framework to labor market data from other developed and developing economies.
ideas.sourceTitle: The Cyclical Behovior of the Gross Flows of U.S. Workers

ideas.title: Incorporate additional structural shocks into the model
ideas.description: Identify and analyze the effects of other potential structural shocks, like policy or demographic shocks, on worker flows.
ideas.sourceTitle: The Cyclical Behovior of the Gross Flows of U.S. Workers

ideas.title: Investigate time-varying importance of identified structural shocks
ideas.description: Examine whether the relative significance of profitability, job-specific, and search shocks changes across different economic eras.
ideas.sourceTitle: The Cyclical Behovior of the Gross Flows of U.S. Workers

ideas.title: News vs. Sunspots in Surplus Country Adjustment: An Empirical DSGE Framework for the Eurozone
ideas.description: This research develops and estimates a two-country DSGE model of the eurozone core-periphery to empirically distinguish between news shocks and sunspot-driven equilibria as explanations for the persistent external surpluses and domestic demand weakness in a surplus country like Germany. The empirical strategy will test if observed macroeconomic dynamics are consistent with forward-looking fundamentals (e.g., anticipated demographic change or technological leadership) or with coordination on pessimistic sunspot equilibria (e.g., expectations of perpetual austerity or currency union fragility). The results would provide a structural, micro-founded account of whether surplus country constraints are a rational response to news or a source of inefficient equilibrium selection, with direct implications for the design of eurozone fiscal and macroeconomic stabilization policies.

ideas.title: Scale up to a global panel of countries
ideas.description: Estimate a multi-country panel SVAR to identify global versus country-specific structural shocks to labor markets.
ideas.sourceTitle: The Cyclical Behovior of the Gross Flows of U.S. Workers

ideas.title: Study the dual problem of vacancy duration and flows.
ideas.description: Analyze the duration of job vacancies and vacancy creation/destruction flows symmetrically to unemployment.
ideas.sourceTitle: What Hides Behind an Unemployment Rate: Comparing Portuguese and U.S. Labor Markets

ideas.title: Inverse Observational Equivalence and Regional Imbalances: Identifying News Shocks in Portuguese Regional Adjustment
ideas.description: This research applies the inverse observational equivalence mapping to the adjustment dynamics of a Portuguese region (Algarve or Norte), treating it as a small open economy within the national framework. It seeks to identify which observed patterns of regional external and internal imbalances (e.g., current account, unemployment, sectoral shifts) can be uniquely mapped to a structural model driven by news shocks about future national or European policy, rather than sunspots or traditional fundamental shocks. The regional context provides specific institutional and structural details that help discipline the otherwise indeterminate mapping, offering a new method to empirically identify the role of anticipatory dynamics in regional divergence and stabilization.

ideas.title: Institutional Preconditions for Non-Devaluation Adjustment in a Monetary Union
ideas.description: This research investigates the specific institutional reforms required within a currency union, like the Eurozone, to make demand-side or coordinated wage-led strategies viable for restoring competitiveness. It moves beyond asking if alternatives exist to asking under what precise institutional configurations they could function, treating the current union's architecture as the core constraint. The methodology employs comparative political economy, analyzing historical and theoretical monetary unions, and builds formal models to test the stability of alternative adjustment mechanisms under different fiscal, banking, and political integration schemes. The output is a set of necessary and sufficient institutional conditions for escaping the internal devaluation imperative, providing a roadmap for structural reform rather than just policy alternatives.

ideas.title: Surplus Country Adjustment Under Asymmetric Information: A Real-Time Policy Dilemma
ideas.description: This research investigates the adjustment problem of a surplus country like Germany within a monetary union, but under the critical assumption that its fiscal and regulatory authorities possess superior, real-time information about the state of domestic demand and inflationary pressures compared to the common central bank (ECB) and other member states. The core question becomes how this informational advantage influences the timing and credibility of the surplus country's promised fiscal expansion or structural reforms, which are demanded by the union to rebalance the economy. The model would explore strategic delays, where Germany might postpone adjustment based on its private signal of weak domestic demand, creating a new source of policy conflict and persistence in intra-union imbalances that is invisible to standard econometric models using lagged data.

ideas.title: Nonlinear Rational Expectations and Structural Reform: Quantifying Productivity Impacts with Non-Gaussian Shocks and Multiple Equilibria
ideas.description: This research investigates the empirical impact of structural reforms on Portugal's tradable sector productivity within a nonlinear rational expectations framework. It tests whether the estimated effects are sensitive to non-Gaussian shocks (e.g., fat-tailed or skewed distributions common in crises) and the possibility of multiple equilibria driven by endogenous confidence. The core innovation is to determine if the observational equivalence of different reform types breaks down under nonlinearities, revealing which reforms are robust to extreme events and which might trigger virtuous or vicious cycles through expectation channels.

ideas.title: Scale down to local labor market analysis
ideas.description: Apply the structural shock framework to metropolitan or regional data to study local labor market dynamics.
ideas.sourceTitle: The Cyclical Behovior of the Gross Flows of U.S. Workers

ideas.title: Study the dual problem of firm job flows
ideas.description: Apply the same shock identification logic to data on job creation and destruction rates at firms.
ideas.sourceTitle: The Cyclical Behovior of the Gross Flows of U.S. Workers

ideas.title: Regional Stress-Testing for Optimal Currency Union Entry: A Microcosm Framework from Portugal
ideas.description: This research uses a detailed case study of a Portuguese region (e.g., Algarve or Norte) experiencing Portugal's post-euro imbalances as a microcosm to inversely engineer optimal entry criteria. By analyzing the region's sectoral composition, factor mobility, and institutional capacity for adjustment, the study identifies which pre-entry economic conditions at the national level could have mitigated the region's specific vulnerabilities. The result is a regionally-grounded, empirically validated framework that moves beyond one-size-fits-all Maastricht criteria to propose asymmetric and structural pre-conditions for future euro adopters, tailored to diverse economic architectures within candidate countries.

ideas.title: Quantify productivity impact of proposed structural reforms
ideas.description: Empirically estimate the potential effects of specific structural reforms on Portugal's tradable sector productivity.
ideas.sourceTitle: Adjustment within the euro. The difficult case of Portugal

ideas.title: Adaptive Learning and Regime Uncertainty in a Small Open Economy with Contingent Currency Regimes
ideas.description: This research integrates adaptive learning dynamics into a macroeconomic model for Portugal where euro membership is not a fixed, permanent assumption but a contingent policy state. Agents form expectations about inflation, output, and interest rates not only based on current policy but also on their evolving beliefs about the probability and consequences of a regime shift to a new currency. The core novelty is examining how the learning process itself interacts with the possibility of exit, potentially creating self-fulfilling dynamics, hysteresis effects in expectations, and non-linear transitions that rational expectations would preclude. This allows for a richer analysis of stability, the credibility of fiscal and monetary frameworks, and the real-time formation of exit premia in financial markets.

ideas.title: Link identified shocks to specific observable economic events
ideas.description: Correlate the estimated structural shock series with historical episodes like recessions or policy reforms for narrative validation.
ideas.sourceTitle: The Cyclical Behovior of the Gross Flows of U.S. Workers

ideas.title: Nonlinear Observational Equivalence and the Design of Robust Euro Entry Criteria
ideas.description: This research investigates the design of optimal euro entry criteria through the lens of nonlinear rational expectations models. It asks whether the standard Maastricht criteria, or a proposed optimal set of pre-conditions, are uniquely determined by observable economic outcomes when the underlying structural model is nonlinear, relaxing the typical linearity assumption. The core analysis tests for 'observational equivalence' in this nonlinear setting: could different sets of entry rules produce the same observable path for a candidate country, thereby making the optimal policy non-identifiable? The goal is to derive entry criteria that are not only theoretically optimal but also robust to this fundamental identification problem inherent in complex, nonlinear economies.

ideas.title: Challenge exogeneity of matching efficiency to other shocks
ideas.description: Model matching efficiency as endogenous, potentially responding to aggregate or job-specific economic conditions.
ideas.sourceTitle: The Cyclical Behovior of the Gross Flows of U.S. Workers

ideas.title: Labor demand is static in the model
ideas.description: Incorporate dynamic labor demand and capital adjustment in response to wage hysteresis.
ideas.sourceTitle: Hysteresis and the European Unemployment Problem

ideas.title: Sunspots, Sentiment, and Stabilization: Mapping Indeterminate Beliefs to Optimal Fiscal Transfers in a Model Eurozone Federation
ideas.description: This research models the eurozone as a fiscal federation where regional business cycles can be driven by indeterminate equilibria and self-fulfilling shifts in investor sentiment (sunspots). It applies the inverse observational equivalence mapping to identify which observed fluctuations in a region like Portugal could be rationalized as either fundamental news shocks or non-fundamental sunspot shocks. The core innovation is to then compute the design of an optimal federal fiscal transfer system that specifically targets and stabilizes the region against the welfare-reducing volatility originating from this belief-driven indeterminacy, thereby severing the sunspot-to-outcome link.

ideas.title: Compare transition economies with other developing nations
ideas.description: Extend the analysis to compare post-Communist financial repression with that in other developing regions.
ideas.sourceTitle: The Economics Of Post-Communist Transition

ideas.title: Apply network theory to eurozone imbalances
ideas.description: Use network analysis to map financial and trade flows creating Portugal's deficits within the eurozone.
ideas.sourceTitle: Adjustment within the euro. The difficult case of Portugal

ideas.title: Use machine learning to model large residual variance
ideas.description: Apply ML techniques to model the high residual volatility in the inflation-unemployment relation.
ideas.sourceTitle: The Phillips Curve: Back to the '60s?

ideas.title: The Political Economy of Immovable Surpluses: Veto Points, Interest Coalitions, and the German Rebalancing Impasse
ideas.description: This research systematically investigates why eurozone surplus countries, using Germany as the pivotal case, fail to adjust despite systemic pressure. It moves beyond macroeconomic symmetry to analyze the domestic political economy, identifying the specific institutional veto points (constitutional debt brakes, coalition politics, central bank influence) and the coalition of export-oriented firms, fiscal conservatives, and skeptical publics that sustain surplus policies. The study employs comparative historical analysis of adjustment episodes and counterfactual policy scenarios to determine which constraints are fundamental and whether external incentives or internal shifts could alter the political equilibrium blocking rebalancing.

ideas.title: Apply epidemiological models to wage-price rigidity
ideas.description: Model the diffusion of wage cuts and price changes across the economy using contagion models.
ideas.sourceTitle: Adjustment within the euro. The difficult case of Portugal

ideas.title: Machine Learning Classification of Macroeconomic Adjustment Regimes in the Eurozone Periphery
ideas.description: This research applies machine learning techniques for model discrimination to the comparative study of eurozone peripheral economies (Portugal, Ireland, Greece, Spain). Instead of manually classifying adjustment episodes, we train classifiers (e.g., SVMs, random forests, or neural networks) on simulated time-series data from competing macroeconomic models—such as fundamental 'news' models versus multiple-equilibria 'sunspot' models—to identify which theoretical mechanism best explains the observed empirical trajectories of key variables (sovereign spreads, growth, unemployment) in each country during critical periods. This provides a rigorous, quantitative basis for comparing adjustment regimes across countries, testing whether different peripherals experienced fundamentally different types of crises, and assessing the relative empirical relevance of competing crisis theories in a unified framework.

ideas.title: A Minimal Univariate Model of Self-Fulfilling Euro Entry Criteria
ideas.description: This research constructs the simplest possible univariate model to demonstrate how the optimal economic pre-conditions for euro adoption are not purely technocratic but are fundamentally co-determined by expectations. The model posits a single key state variable (e.g., a composite fiscal-monetary index) and shows how its equilibrium path bifurcates based on extrinsic market beliefs (sunspots) about future adherence. This formalizes the 'news-sunspot equivalence' within the specific institutional context of a currency union entry rule, revealing that even a country satisfying all nominal criteria can be driven onto a suboptimal post-entry trajectory solely by a shift in expectations regarding its future flexibility.

ideas.title: Analyze sectoral differences in job creation and destruction.
ideas.description: Investigate if employment protection affects job flows uniformly across different economic sectors.
ideas.sourceTitle: What Hides Behind an Unemployment Rate: Comparing Portuguese and U.S. Labor Markets

ideas.title: Testing for Non-Fundamentalness in Boom-Bust Narratives: A Causal Inversion Framework
ideas.description: This research synthesizes the statistical toolkit for detecting non-fundamental moving averages with the substantive economic hypothesis of inverted causality in boom-bust cycles. It proposes applying tests for non-fundamentalness to structural vector autoregressive (SVAR) models of a specific economic episode (e.g., a Euro-entry boom and slump) to determine if the data's informational structure is consistent with the 'sunspot' narrative of self-fulfilling optimism or the alternative narrative of pre-existing structural weaknesses driving the cycle. This approach provides a formal, econometric method to adjudicate between competing causal explanations by testing which proposed shock series is fundamental (i.e., recoverable from the observed data history) and which is not, thereby offering direct evidence on the direction of causality embedded in the economic time series.

ideas.title: Comparative Analysis of Eurozone Peripherals Using Ecological Resilience and Regime Shift Frameworks
ideas.description: This research adapts the concept of ecological resilience and regime shifts from ecosystem science to conduct a comparative case study of adjustment mechanisms in Portugal, Ireland, Greece, and Spain post-euro entry. The analogous solution involves treating each national economy as a complex system subjected to a major shock (the financial/eurozone crisis), akin to an ecosystem facing a drought or fire. The study will identify and track key economic and institutional 'state variables'—such as fiscal balances, external competitiveness, banking sector health, and labor market flexibility—to analyze pre-crisis vulnerabilities, the crisis tipping point, and the post-crisis adjustment trajectory. The goal is to systematically classify the different recovery 'pathways' (e.g., rapid export-led rebound, prolonged depression with structural reform, bailout-dependent restructuring) and isolate the institutional and policy factors that acted as 'resilience buffers' or 'amplifiers of stress,' explaining why ostensibly similar peripherals had such divergent outcomes.

ideas.title: Apply survival analysis to unemployment duration data.
ideas.description: Use survival analysis from biostatistics to model the hazard rate of exiting unemployment.
ideas.sourceTitle: What Hides Behind an Unemployment Rate: Comparing Portuguese and U.S. Labor Markets

ideas.title: Incorregate international trade and capital flows
ideas.description: Open the model economy to foreign direct investment and export markets.
ideas.sourceTitle: On the Speed of Transition in Central Europe

ideas.title: Modeling cross-country variations in transition speed
ideas.description: Formally model and test the causes of differences in transition outcomes across Central European countries.
ideas.sourceTitle: On the Speed of Transition in Central Europe

ideas.title: Model monetary policy with anchored expectations and large residuals
ideas.description: Develop new monetary policy frameworks accounting for well-anchored inflation expectations and high residual volatility.
ideas.sourceTitle: The Phillips Curve: Back to the '60s?

ideas.title: Econometric Testing and Sufficient Statistics for Identifying Lagged Expectations in Macroeconomic Models
ideas.description: This research develops a practical econometric framework to empirically distinguish models where agents form expectations based on lagged information from alternative models of expectations formation and other rigidities. It derives the unique set of cross-equation restrictions or moment inequalities that are necessary and sufficient for identification of the lagged expectations mechanism, moving beyond theoretical discussion. The output is a suite of specification tests and a 'sufficient statistics' toolkit that applied economists can use with common macroeconomic datasets to validate or reject the lagged expectations assumption in their models, thereby grounding the theoretical identification problem in empirical practice.

ideas.title: Conditional Euro Exit Scenarios: An Early-Warning Trigger Framework for Portugal
ideas.description: This research constructs a multi-stage model where the relaxation of fixed euro membership is not an unconditional assumption but a probabilistic outcome triggered by quantifiable crisis indicators. The first stage employs a Bayesian early-warning model, calibrated on historical episodes of monetary union stress and exit, to identify the specific economic and political conditions that would make a Portuguese exit a non-negligible risk. The second stage only activates the exit or multi-currency regime scenarios when simulated or observed data crosses these pre-defined threat thresholds, ensuring scenario relevance. The final output is a set of conditional, stress-tested economic pathways that are rigorously anchored to credible crisis mechanics rather than abstract speculation.

ideas.title: Formally model interest rates and sales persistence
ideas.description: Develop a structural model to formally test the paper's suggested link between interest rate behavior and reduced sales autocorrelation.
ideas.sourceTitle: The Production and Inventory Behavior of the American Automobile Industry

ideas.title: Relax homogeneous labor assumption
ideas.description: Introduce worker skill heterogeneity and retraining into the two-sector transition model.
ideas.sourceTitle: On the Speed of Transition in Central Europe

ideas.title: Identification and Expectations in Non-Tradable Productivity-Driven Adjustment
ideas.description: This research investigates the identification challenges in open-economy models when the standard focus on the tradable sector may be insufficient. It specifically examines scenarios where a sustained increase in non-tradable services productivity presents a theoretically viable adjustment path. The core analysis centers on how agents forming expectations with lagged information can create identification problems, potentially obscuring this alternative path. The synergy lies in determining whether observed macroeconomic dynamics are uniquely attributable to tradable-sector shocks or could also be consistent with an equilibrium driven by lagged expectations about a non-tradable productivity transition, with critical implications for policy design.

ideas.title: Scale analysis to sub-national regional labor markets.
ideas.description: Compare flow and duration dynamics between regions within a single country with uniform national laws.
ideas.sourceTitle: What Hides Behind an Unemployment Rate: Comparing Portuguese and U.S. Labor Markets

ideas.title: Model worker flows beyond three-state framework
ideas.description: Incorporate more labor market states, like underemployment or labor force entry/exit, into the shock identification scheme.
ideas.sourceTitle: The Cyclical Behovior of the Gross Flows of U.S. Workers

ideas.title: Quantifying the Political Salience of Pre-Crisis Structural Weaknesses in Eurozone Boom-Bust Narratives
ideas.description: This research applies NLP to a corpus of media, parliamentary, and central bank texts from the pre-crisis boom period in specific Eurozone periphery countries. It quantifies the political salience and consensus around pre-existing structural weaknesses (e.g., low productivity, rigid markets) versus the salience of euro-entry optimism narratives. By analyzing the temporal evolution and relative weight of these discourses, the study directly tests the inverted causality hypothesis: if structural weaknesses were a primary driver, they should have been prominently discussed as risks *during* the boom, not just after the bust. The synergy provides a data-driven method to move beyond theoretical causality claims and map the actual policy awareness landscape that preceded the crisis.

ideas.title: Invert causality: volatility caused policy change
ideas.description: Explore if the Great Moderation itself caused changes in monetary policy, rather than interest rates causing sales changes.
ideas.sourceTitle: The Production and Inventory Behavior of the American Automobile Industry

ideas.title: Scale across time: Long-run effects of transition repression
ideas.description: Track the long-term economic and political consequences of early transition-era financial repression.
ideas.sourceTitle: The Economics Of Post-Communist Transition

ideas.title: Scale up: Model eurozone as a federal system
ideas.description: Treat the eurozone as a fiscal federation and model optimal transfers to Portugal-like regions.
ideas.sourceTitle: Adjustment within the euro. The difficult case of Portugal

ideas.title: Empirical Quantification of Observational Equivalence in Medium-Scale Nonlinear DSGE Models
ideas.description: This research direction proposes to develop and estimate medium-scale nonlinear DSGE models to quantify the empirical relevance of observational equivalence. It moves beyond theoretical explorations of nonlinearities and small-scale linear estimations by integrating both challenges. The core objective is to use modern estimation techniques on macroeconomic data to measure the practical identifiability issues—such as distinguishing between policy rule changes and shock processes—that may arise or be mitigated by the model's nonlinear structure. This provides a crucial bridge between theoretical equivalence results and their implications for policy analysis using state-of-the-art empirical models.

ideas.title: Assess long-term impacts of unbundling and privatization
ideas.description: Quantitatively evaluate the long-term effects of different unbundling and privatization models on prices and investment.
ideas.sourceTitle: Reform in Eastern Europe

ideas.title: Assume job creation and destruction are independent of flows.
ideas.description: Explore if factors like technology shocks drive job flows independently of worker reallocation decisions.
ideas.sourceTitle: What Hides Behind an Unemployment Rate: Comparing Portuguese and U.S. Labor Markets

ideas.title: Dual problem: hysteresis in inflation or participation rate
ideas.description: Apply the insider-outsider persistence logic to inflation expectations or labor force participation.
ideas.sourceTitle: Hysteresis and the European Unemployment Problem

ideas.title: Scale up: Model multi-region transition with migration
ideas.description: Extend the model to multiple regions with internal labor migration.
ideas.sourceTitle: On the Speed of Transition in Central Europe

ideas.title: Extend anchored expectations analysis to other advanced economies
ideas.description: Examine if the trend of increasingly anchored inflation expectations is a global phenomenon.
ideas.sourceTitle: The Phillips Curve: Back to the '60s?

ideas.title: Impedance Matching for Economic Surplus: A Control Systems Approach to Eurozone Rebalancing
ideas.description: This research seed applies an analogy from electrical engineering and control systems to the symmetric problem of adjustment in a eurozone surplus country like Germany. The core problem resembles an impedance mismatch in a circuit, where Germany's high-saving, high-trade-surplus economy does not efficiently transfer demand to deficit member states, causing systemic strain and suboptimal power (economic performance) for the union. The analogous solution is impedance matching, where a network of components is designed to transform the source impedance to match the load. Adapted to this context, the research will investigate the design of coordinated policy 'matching networks'—potentially involving fiscal tools, investment conduits, and structural policies—to transform Germany's surplus (savings) into effective demand for goods and services from deficit countries. The goal is to outline a framework for rebalancing that maximizes the stability and growth transfer within the eurozone system while minimizing disruptive political and economic feedback, effectively solving the dual problem of surplus country constraints through a systems engineering lens.

ideas.title: Agent-Based Sunspot-to-News Mapping for Sectoral Adjustment
ideas.description: This research develops an agent-based computational economics (ACE) framework to simulate firm and household behavior during an internal devaluation, explicitly modeling the coordination problem inherent in sectoral wage and price adjustment. The key innovation is to use the detailed simulation output to perform an 'inverse observational equivalence' analysis: by applying econometric techniques for news/sunspot identification to the artificial ACE data, we can map the simulated aggregate outcomes back to the specific micro-level coordination mechanisms and belief-formation rules that generated them. This allows for a controlled, structural investigation into which types of agent interactions and information structures make a sunspot-driven recession versus a news-driven recession more likely, providing new micro-founded insights into the nature of macroeconomic indeterminacy.

ideas.title: Scale up to supranational governance bodies
ideas.description: Apply the framework to the European Union's relationship with member states regarding economic policy.
ideas.sourceTitle: Federalism with and without Political Centralization: China Versus Russia

ideas.title: Political institutions primarily shape financial policy
ideas.description: Challenge the primacy of political institutions by testing economic crisis or external pressure as drivers.
ideas.sourceTitle: The Economics Of Post-Communist Transition

ideas.title: Mapping the Political Feasibility of Surplus Adjustment: A Textual Analysis of German Policy Discourse
ideas.description: This research applies NLP techniques to a large corpus of German media, parliamentary debates, and government statements to quantify the political consensus or contention surrounding specific policy options for reducing Germany's current account surplus. By analyzing the framing, sentiment, and coalitional support for different adjustment mechanisms (e.g., fiscal expansion, wage increases, structural reforms), it maps the domestic political feasibility landscape. This provides an empirical foundation for understanding why certain economically prescribed adjustments are politically stalled, offering a symmetric political economy analysis to complement the macroeconomic study of surplus country constraints.

ideas.title: Assess dynamic impacts of policy shocks via variance decomposition
ideas.description: Use variance decomposition functions to quantify the importance of different structural shocks.
ideas.sourceTitle: A Traditional Interpretation of Macroeconomic Fluctuations

ideas.title: Adapting Epidemiological Compartmental Models for Sectoral Adjustment Dynamics
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution to a classic problem in epidemiology—modeling disease propagation—to the economic problem of sectoral adjustment. In epidemiology, compartmental models (like SIR) successfully simulate complex population dynamics by defining states and transition rates between them. The analogous solution is adapted by redefining the compartments as economic states (e.g., solvent, distressed, bankrupt, adjusted) for firms and households within an agent-based model. The 'transmission' rates become probabilities of financial distress spreading through inter-agent networks (trade credit, employment), and policy interventions act as modulators of these rates. This framework allows for simulating the cascading, non-linear adjustment paths during an internal devaluation, testing how the timing and targeting of austerity or support policies affect the overall economic 'recovery curve' across sectors.

ideas.title: Model long-term growth under fiscal consolidation scenarios
ideas.description: Formally model Portugal's potential output growth under different fiscal adjustment paths and their impact on debt sustainability.
ideas.sourceTitle: Adjustment within the euro. The difficult case of Portugal

ideas.title: Robustness of Federal Transfers: Testing Solution Methods for a Fiscal Federation RE Model
ideas.description: This research constructs a rational expectations model of a fiscal federation, like the eurozone, with optimal transfer rules to asymmetric regions. It then deliberately solves the model using multiple alternative methods (e.g., parameterized expectations, projection methods, perturbation at different orders) beyond the standard martingale difference approach. The core analysis examines whether the recommended scale and dynamics of federal transfers—and their stated stabilization benefits—are robust across solution techniques, or if policy conclusions are an artifact of a specific computational assumption.

ideas.title: Comparative Regional Adjustment: A Paired Case Study of Structural Heterogeneity in Portugal
ideas.description: This research employs a comparative case study of two structurally distinct Portuguese regions, such as the tourism-dependent Algarve and the more industrialized Norte, to analyze economic adjustment. By systematically comparing how external and internal imbalances manifest and are corrected in these contrasting contexts, the study moves beyond treating a single region as a representative microcosm. This paired design allows for the identification of adjustment mechanisms that are generalizable to the national level versus those that are idiosyncratic to specific regional economic structures. The analysis thereby provides a more robust, nuanced understanding of Portugal's aggregate adjustment challenges by explicitly accounting for internal heterogeneity.

ideas.title: Modeling the Structural-Sentiment Feedback Loop in Euro-Periphery Boom-Bust Cycles
ideas.description: This research investigates how pre-existing structural weaknesses and euro-entry optimism interacted recursively to generate unsustainable booms and severe slumps in the euro periphery. It uses detailed case studies to map the mechanisms through which structural flaws (e.g., in financial supervision) amplified optimistic capital inflows, which in turn further weakened structures by masking risks. A quantitative panel analysis then tests for and measures the strength of these feedback effects, moving beyond causal primacy to explain the cycle's explosive dynamics.

ideas.title: Inverse problem: from observed flows to shock prevalence
ideas.description: Develop a method to infer the historical prevalence of structural shocks from observed patterns in worker flow volatilities.
ideas.sourceTitle: The Cyclical Behovior of the Gross Flows of U.S. Workers

ideas.title: An Agent-Based Network Model of Eurozone Imbalances: Simulating the Causal Dynamics of Portugal's Deficits
ideas.description: This research integrates network theory with agent-based modeling to move beyond static mapping and uncover the causal dynamics of eurozone imbalances. It builds a multi-layer network of trade and financial flows and populates it with heterogeneous, adaptive agents whose interactions are governed by empirically-grounded behavioral rules. The model simulates how strategic behaviors and network feedback loops generate Portugal's persistent deficits, enabling counterfactual analysis of proposed fiscal, banking, and structural policies to assess their systemic impact on imbalance reduction.

ideas.title: Empirical tests on industry-level insider power
ideas.description: Test the theory using cross-industry data on unionization, turnover, and unemployment persistence.
ideas.sourceTitle: Hysteresis and the European Unemployment Problem

ideas.title: Use matching theory for labor reallocation frictions
ideas.description: Incorporate search and matching frictions into the labor reallocation process.
ideas.sourceTitle: On the Speed of Transition in Central Europe

ideas.title: Inverse Design of Euro Entry: A Biomechanical Stress-Testing Analogy
ideas.description: This research adapts a solution from biomechanical engineering to the economic problem of designing optimal euro entry criteria. The analogous problem is designing a prosthetic implant that must endure long-term mechanical stress without failing. Engineers solve this using inverse finite element analysis: they begin with a known failure mode (implant loosening) and computationally work backward to derive the optimal material properties and preoperative bone conditions required for success. Adapting this, we start from the observed 'failure mode' of Portugal's post-entry economic difficulties (e.g., loss of competitiveness, growth stagnation under a one-size-fits-all monetary policy). We then treat the eurozone as a complex system applying economic 'stress' (asymmetric shocks, fixed exchange rates). Using computational economic modeling and inverse problem-solving techniques, we derive the optimal set of pre-entry economic conditions—such as precise thresholds for labor market flexibility, product market integration, export structure resilience, and fiscal buffer capacity—that would make a future adopter robust to these stresses, effectively creating a scientifically-derived, stress-tested entry criteria set.

ideas.title: Study the inverse: disintegration of existing unions
ideas.description: Analyze the breakdown of unions as the dual process to their formation presented in the paper.
ideas.sourceTitle: Regional Evolutions

ideas.title: Dual problem: Study surplus country constraints
ideas.description: Investigate the symmetric problem of adjustment in Germany, a eurozone surplus country.
ideas.sourceTitle: Adjustment within the euro. The difficult case of Portugal

ideas.title: Modeling External Viability: Non-Tradable Productivity Growth under Balance-of-Payments Constraints
ideas.description: This research investigates whether targeted productivity growth in the non-tradable services sector can serve as a sustainable engine for macroeconomic adjustment without provoking a balance-of-payments crisis. It employs a structural two-sector model to trace how gains in non-tradable efficiency affect the real exchange rate, domestic demand, and the tradable sector's response. The core analysis identifies the precise threshold conditions—such as the elasticity of substitution in demand and the mobility of factors—under which non-tradable-led growth can generate sufficient indirect tradable sector dynamism or capital inflows to meet external obligations. The outcome is a contingency framework that specifies when this alternative path is viable versus when it remains insufficient without complementary tradable sector policies.

ideas.title: Model political incentives shaping financial policy
ideas.description: Develop formal models of how political institutions create incentives for repressive financial controls.
ideas.sourceTitle: The Economics Of Post-Communist Transition

ideas.title: Using SVAR Sign Restrictions to Empirically Bound the Class of Equivalent Indeterminate Models
ideas.description: This research synthesizes the formal characterization of the class of observationally equivalent indeterminate models (like news shocks models) with the empirical toolkit of Structural VAR identification. It proposes using sign and zero restrictions—standard tools for identifying structural shocks in SVARs—not to identify a single model, but to empirically delineate the subset of theoretically equivalent models that are also consistent with established macro-dynamic sign patterns. This transforms the abstract equivalence class into an empirically bounded set, allowing researchers to assess which members of the class are empirically plausible and to test the limits of observational equivalence using data.

ideas.title: Modeling Eurozone Transfers as a Biological Homeostatic System
ideas.description: This idea draws an analogy from systems physiology, where homeostatic mechanisms like blood pressure regulation maintain stability through sensor-feedback-effector loops. The analogous solution involves automatic, rule-based responses to deviations from a physiological set point. Adapted to the research context, the eurozone is treated as a federal organism. The project would model optimal fiscal transfers by defining clear macroeconomic stability indicators (set points) for member states, designing a central fiscal capacity (regulatory center) that automatically disburses funds based on real-time deviations from these indicators, much like a homeostatic system. This creates a rule-based, depoliticized transfer mechanism aimed at maintaining the equilibrium of the monetary union.

ideas.title: Solve the inverse problem: from shocks to theory
ideas.description: Infer the most likely theoretical restrictions from the observed shock propagation patterns.
ideas.sourceTitle: A Traditional Interpretation of Macroeconomic Fluctuations

ideas.title: Applying Network Immunization Strategies to Wage-Price Rigidity
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from network epidemiology to the problem of wage-price rigidity. In epidemiology, targeted immunization of high-degree hub nodes is a proven strategy for efficiently containing contagion. The analogous economic problem is the slow, sticky diffusion of wage and price adjustments across an interdependent economy. The adapted research idea is to model the economy as a network of firms and sectors, apply network analysis to identify critical hubs (e.g., major employers, benchmark price setters), and design targeted policy interventions at these hubs. The goal is to disrupt the 'contagion' of rigidity or, conversely, to strategically propagate necessary adjustments, offering a more efficient alternative to economy-wide measures for managing inflation or deflation dynamics.

ideas.title: Applying Ecological Food Web Stability Analysis to Eurozone Financial-Trade Networks
ideas.description: This research adapts the methodology used by ecologists to study the stability of food webs to analyze eurozone imbalances. In ecology, networks of species interactions are analyzed using metrics like connectance and interaction strength to predict cascading effects and identify keystone species whose removal destabilizes the system. The adapted idea applies this to a dual-layer network model of the eurozone, combining financial flows (e.g., Target2 balances, cross-border banking claims) and trade flows (goods and services). By treating countries as nodes and financial/trade imbalances as directed, weighted links, the research will identify systemically important 'keystone' economies and financial channels. It will apply ecological network stability metrics to simulate how deficits in a country like Portugal originate and propagate, and to assess the overall resilience of the eurozone network to financial shocks and policy interventions.

ideas.title: Modeling the Contagion of Wage-Price Rigidity as a Function of Political Discourse
ideas.description: This research integrates epidemiological models of wage-price rigidity with NLP analysis of policy discourse to create a novel framework. It treats the diffusion of wage cuts and price changes as a contagion process whose transmission rate is dynamically modulated by the level of political consensus extracted from media and parliamentary texts. This allows the model to simulate how shifts in public debate and policy uncertainty can accelerate or stall economic adjustment, moving beyond static models. The synergy provides a causal pathway to test how political narrative directly influences the real economy's speed of adjustment to shocks.

ideas.title: Applying Quantum Decoherence Methods to Rational Expectations Model Solution Equivalence
ideas.description: This idea challenges the standard martingale difference solution approach in rational expectations models by drawing an analogy from quantum mechanics, specifically the solution to the quantum measurement problem via decoherence. In quantum theory, decoherence explains the emergence of a definite classical state from a superposition without a unique collapse axiom, and different mathematical formulations of this process are analyzed for equivalence. Adapted to economics, the research would apply analogous alternative mathematical frameworks from dynamical systems—such as ergodic theory or invariant manifold analysis—to model the selection of an economic path from a set of rational expectations equilibria. The core investigation is to determine the formal conditions for equivalence between these methods and the traditional martingale approach, and to identify scenarios where the choice of solution method leads to divergent economic forecasts or policy conclusions, thereby probing the robustness of standard macroeconomic modeling assumptions.

ideas.title: Applying Climate Model Downscaling Techniques to Validate DSGE Equivalence
ideas.description: This research seed proposes to quantify the empirical relevance of theoretical equivalences in large-scale DSGE models by drawing an analogy from climate science validation techniques. In climate modeling, the empirical credibility of large-scale Global Circulation Models (GCMs) is often tested through dynamical downscaling: using GCM outputs as boundary conditions for higher-resolution Regional Climate Models (RCMs), whose detailed predictions can be directly compared to local observational data. We adapt this solution by constructing medium-scale 'test-bed' DSGE models that are designed to isolate and embody the specific theoretical equivalence of interest (e.g., Ricardian equivalence, monetary policy neutrality propositions). These tractable models are then estimated using standard macroeconomic time series data. The core analysis involves testing whether the estimated parameters and model dynamics are consistent with the imposed theoretical equivalence, effectively 'downscaling' the large-scale theoretical question to a medium-scale empirical validation exercise. This approach provides a rigorous, focused method to assess which theoretical equivalences hold empirical weight, informing future development of both large-scale and medium-scale macroeconomic models.

ideas.title: Structural Identification of Agent Information Sets from Discrepancies in Micro-Actions and Aggregate Data
ideas.description: This research develops a framework to structurally identify the specific variables in agents' information sets that are missing from the econometrician's standard data. It uses granular micro-data on agent actions (e.g., firm investment, household consumption) alongside traditional aggregate series to pinpoint systematic forecasting advantages. The model explicitly tests competing hypotheses about the source of superior information—such as local conditions, private signals, or processing of public data—and estimates the economic impact of this informational asymmetry. The output is a falsifiable theory of informational edges, moving beyond a mere assumption to a measurable component of agent behavior.

ideas.title: Modeling Dynamic Feedback: A Real-Time Political Economy Analysis of Fiscal Consolidation Signals in Portugal
ideas.description: This research moves beyond static feasibility assessments to model the dynamic political feedback loops triggered by fiscal adjustment proposals in Portugal. It employs a mixed-methods design combining computational analysis of media and social media discourse, panel survey experiments gauging public reaction to specific policy trade-offs, and repeated elite interviews to track how political strategies evolve in response to societal signals. The goal is to create a process-oriented model that explains not just which policies are theoretically feasible, but how their political viability changes through the cycle of announcement, societal reaction, and political recalibration.

ideas.title: Disciplined Adaptive Learning: A Micro-Founded Test of Policy Invariance
ideas.description: This research program rigorously tests the Lucas Critique by replacing rational expectations with a specific, empirically disciplined adaptive learning process. The learning rule is a real-time recursive estimator, with its key parameters (like the gain) structurally estimated to replicate the actual forecast error correction dynamics observed in professional survey data. The core experiment is to then examine whether foundational macroeconomic policy invariance results (like the Taylor principle or fiscal multiplier equivalence) hold under this micro-founded learning process. This approach moves beyond abstract 'learning' to provide a concrete, empirically accountable challenge to the rational expectations equilibrium assumption.

ideas.title: An Agent-Based Network Model of Strategic Wage-Price Adjustment Diffusion
ideas.description: This research develops an agent-based computational model to study the diffusion of wage and price changes across a networked economy. Firms and workers are modeled as heterogeneous agents with strategic objectives, embedded in a realistic input-output and labor market network structure. Their decisions to adjust wages or prices are conditional on the observed actions of connected agents, their own financial state, and their market power, moving beyond passive contagion. The model is calibrated with pre-2026 micro-data to simulate how strategic interactions and network topology generate aggregate rigidity or cascading adjustments.

ideas.title: A Pharmacokinetic Model for Simulating the Productivity Impact of Structural Reforms
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution of pharmacokinetic modeling from pharmacology to empirically estimate the potential effects of structural reforms. In drug development, PK/PD models simulate a compound's pathway and effect. Analogously, this project would develop an economic simulation model where proposed reforms are treated as inputs. The model would parameterize the Portuguese tradable sector's 'absorption' capacity (e.g., firm adaptability), 'distribution' channels (e.g., supply chains, labor mobility), and 'metabolic' rates (e.g., speed of competitive response) to forecast the dose-response relationship between specific policy interventions and productivity outcomes, providing a quantified, dynamic impact assessment before implementation.

ideas.title: Modeling Fiscal Consolidation as an Ecological Succession Process
ideas.description: This research adapts models of ecological succession to formally model Portugal's potential output growth under different fiscal consolidation scenarios. Drawing an analogy from ecology, where systems recover from disturbance along predictable but path-dependent trajectories, the economic 'ecosystem' (comprising capital, labor, and productivity) is modeled as interacting state variables with specific recovery dynamics and thresholds. Different fiscal adjustment paths (the 'disturbance regimes') are simulated to project non-linear growth and debt sustainability outcomes. This approach moves beyond linear assumptions to identify critical public investment thresholds and resilient fiscal pathways that prevent long-term stagnation, directly informing the design of consolidation strategies that support sustainable debt reduction.

ideas.title: A Constructive Mapping and Empirical Triangulation of the Indeterminate News Shock Equivalence Class
ideas.description: This research first formally characterizes the full set of indeterminate dynamic models that generate the same aggregate time series as a given news shock model. It then develops an algorithmic procedure to constructively map this equivalence class, parameterizing its dimensions of freedom. Finally, it designs and applies statistical methods—using auxiliary data, narrative restrictions, or micro-evidence—to impose plausible constraints, thereby identifying a empirically relevant and policy-useful subset of models within the theoretically equivalent space.

ideas.title: Using Policy Discourse NLP to Identify and Test for Sunspot Equilibria in Macroeconomic Models
ideas.description: This research applies NLP to media and parliamentary texts to construct time-series indices measuring the coherence, polarization, and narrative content of policy discourse. These indices are then used as observable candidate sunspot variables within an estimated DSGE framework. The core empirical strategy tests whether shifts in these discourse metrics, orthogonal to fundamental news shocks, have significant predictive power for macroeconomic aggregates, thereby providing direct evidence for or against sunspot-driven equilibrium selection. This approach moves beyond abstract theoretical tests by grounding the search for sunspots in the observable dynamics of public political communication.

ideas.title: The Limits of Equivalence: Economic Structures that Fracture Solution Method Consensus in Expectations Models
ideas.description: This research systematically investigates when the martingale difference assumption and alternative rational expectations solution methods fail to produce equivalent economic conclusions. It develops a framework to classify model features—including dispersed information, strategic complementarity, and certain expectational feedback loops—that cause solution paths to diverge across methodologies. The core of the analysis applies a suite of solution techniques to canonical and extended models possessing these features, quantifying the resulting differences in policy functions, impulse responses, and welfare implications. The output is a principled guide for applied researchers on when the choice of solution method carries substantive economic weight, moving beyond a technical equivalence result.

ideas.title: Adapting Ecological Resilience Theory to Economic Competitiveness
ideas.description: This research seed uses an analogy from ecology to challenge the economic assumption that internal devaluation (prolonged wage suppression) is the only path to restore competitiveness in a crisis. The analogous problem in ecology is restoring a collapsed or stressed ecosystem, which is solved not by suppressing a single species but by building systemic resilience through diversification, introducing new beneficial interactions, and enhancing adaptive capacity. Adapting this solution, the research will investigate if alternative demand-side or institutional policies—such as strategic diversification of the economic base, fostering innovation in new high-value sectors, and reforming institutions to be more adaptive—can restore competitiveness without relying on prolonged wage suppression, thereby offering a more resilient and socially sustainable recovery model.

ideas.title: Machine Learning for Discriminating Between Solution Paths in Rational Expectations Models
ideas.description: This research fuses ML-based time series discrimination with the theoretical challenge of solution method equivalence in rational expectations models. It proposes generating simulated time series data from the same fundamental model solved via different methods (e.g., martingale difference versus minimal state variable or forward solution approaches). Advanced ML classifiers are then trained to determine if they can statistically distinguish between the time series generated by these ostensibly equivalent solution paths. A failure to classify successfully provides empirical, data-driven evidence for the practical equivalence of solution methods, while successful discrimination reveals economically meaningful differences obscured by pure theoretical analysis, offering a new validation paradigm for macroeconomic dynamics.

ideas.title: Instrumental Variable Approach for Lagged Expectation Models Using Control Theory Observability Concepts
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution to the 'state observability' problem from control theory to address identification in economic models with lagged expectations. In control theory, when a system's internal states are not directly measurable, engineers use observability tests (e.g., Kalman rank condition) to determine if available output data can uniquely infer those states, often guiding sensor placement. The analogous problem in economics is identifying model parameters when the key variable—agents' expectations—is formed on lagged information and is unobserved. The adapted idea is to formally treat the expectation formation process as a dynamic system with the true expectation as a hidden state. The research will develop a formal 'econometric observability condition' for such models. This condition will specify the necessary properties of the observed data (e.g., characteristics of instrumental variables, exogenous shock processes, or specific dynamic restrictions) required to uniquely identify the model's structural parameters. The outcome is both a theoretical test for identification in this class of models and a practical framework for selecting or validating instruments, directly analogous to choosing measurement points to render a physical system observable.

ideas.title: Inferring Hidden State from Partial Observables: A Signal Processing Approach to Agent Superiority
ideas.description: This idea inverts the standard information timing assumption by drawing an analogy from signal processing and control theory. In those fields, a common problem involves a central estimator (the econometrician) with access only to delayed, noisy measurements, while embedded agents or sensors have access to superior, real-time data about a system's state. The analogous solution uses state-space modeling and filtering techniques (e.g., Kalman filters) to formally account for the dynamics and the disparate information sets, allowing the central observer to infer the hidden state and the agents' real-time information. Adapted to economics, this suggests constructing models where the agent's actions are a function of a latent, high-frequency information process. The econometrician, observing only aggregated outcomes or lagged variables, must use the structure of the model and the agent's revealed actions to jointly estimate the system parameters and the likely real-time information signal driving the agent, thereby challenging the assumption that the modeler's information set is superior or contemporaneous.

ideas.title: Inverse Observational Equivalence via Signal Deconvolution
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution to the blind source separation problem from signal processing to the context of indeterminate sunspot models. In signal processing, Independent Component Analysis (ICA) is used to recover independent source signals from an observed mixture without prior knowledge of the mixing process. Analogously, given an economic model where observed equilibrium outcomes could be generated by either fundamental news shocks or non-fundamental sunspot shocks (making them observationally equivalent), we propose a method to invert this mapping. The adapted idea is to treat the equilibrium time series as the mixed signal and apply a statistical deconvolution technique, akin to ICA, designed for economic models. This technique would seek a transformation that maximizes the statistical distinguishability between the recovered news shock and sunspot shock processes, effectively solving for the unique news shocks model that is observationally equivalent to a given indeterminate sunspot model, thereby scaling the dual problem of identification.

ideas.title: Applying Ecological Niche Theory to Economic Adjustment: A Productivity Diversification Model
ideas.description: This research seed challenges the sufficiency of a tradables-sector focus for economic adjustment by using an analogy from ecological niche theory. In ecology, ecosystem resilience is bolstered not just by keystone species but by the productivity and diversity of supporting species and services. Analogously, this project proposes to examine if boosting productivity in specific non-tradable service sectors can serve as a viable alternative adjustment path for a national economy. The adapted idea involves modeling the economy as an ecosystem to identify non-tradable service 'niches' (e.g., digital infrastructure, education systems) where productivity gains could have high multiplier effects, enhance overall economic resilience to external shocks, and provide a complementary or alternative rebalancing mechanism to traditional export-led strategies.

ideas.title: Adapt Seismology's Blind Deconvolution to Test for Non-Fundamental Sunspot Representations
ideas.description: This idea draws an analogy from geophysics, specifically the blind deconvolution problem in seismology. There, the goal is to separate an unknown source signal from the impulse response of the medium using only the output trace, often relying on statistical assumptions. Similarly, in economics, a non-fundamental moving average representation implies the true economic shock (the 'source') is not recoverable from the observed historical data (the 'trace') due to a non-invertible propagation mechanism (the 'medium response'). We adapt the solution by applying statistical tests—designed to check for the invertibility/minimum-phase property of the moving average—to VAR models of sunspot-driven equilibria. A rejection of fundamentalness would serve as a detection method for sunspot representations, analogous to identifying a non-minimum phase source wavelet in seismic data.

ideas.title: Scale down: Study adjustment in a Portuguese region using ecological resilience modeling
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution from ecosystem ecology, where local ecosystems are studied as microcosms of larger systems using resilience theory and modeling. The analogous solution involves modeling external shocks and internal feedbacks to predict system adjustment. Adapted to the Portuguese context, the study would analyze the Algarve or Norte region as a microcosm of national imbalances by applying ecological resilience frameworks. It would quantify external flows (like tourism revenue or investment) and internal structural feedbacks (like labor market dynamics or public service demand), modeling the region's capacity to adjust to imbalances, identify vulnerabilities, and map potential pathways toward a more resilient socio-economic state.

ideas.title: Adapting Geophysical Signal Separation to Resolve Observational Equivalence in News Shock Estimation
ideas.description: This research seed investigates the problem of observational equivalence in common news shock estimation techniques, where different shock processes can generate statistically similar macroeconomic time paths. Drawing an analogy to geophysics, where seismologists face the analogous problem of disentangling overlapping seismic signals from different sources, we adapt their solution. Geophysicists use dense sensor arrays and blind source separation techniques, constrained by known physical models of wave propagation, to isolate individual events. Adapted to macroeconomics, this suggests developing estimation frameworks that utilize high-dimensional datasets (akin to sensor arrays) and statistical signal processing methods to separate shock components. The key innovation is the formal integration of theory-derived 'physical constraints'—such as specific sign and magnitude restrictions on impulse responses that are unique to news shocks versus noise or other shocks—to provide the necessary identifying information to break the observational equivalence. This approach could lead to more robust empirical procedures for news shock identification.

ideas.title: Adaptive Learning as a Homeostatic Process in Economic Models
ideas.description: This idea adapts the concept of homeostasis from systems physiology to challenge the rational expectations assumption in economics. In physiology, stability is achieved through adaptive, error-correcting feedback, not perfect internal models. The analogous solution is to implement such a feedback-based learning rule for economic agents. The adapted research idea replaces rational expectations with a homeostatic learning algorithm where agents' expectations evolve based on forecast errors, with a variable gain that adjusts to economic volatility. This approach introduces bounded rationality as a dynamic control process, providing a new mechanistic foundation to test whether key theoretical results (like the equivalence of certain fiscal policies) are robust to more realistic, biologically-inspired cognitive constraints.

ideas.title: Interpretable Feature Discovery and Constrained Discrimination for Time Series Models
ideas.description: This research moves beyond black-box classification by explicitly discovering and validating the statistical features that best discriminate between time series from news-driven and sunspot-cycle economic models. The system employs interpretable machine learning techniques, such as symbolic regression and attention mechanisms, to propose candidate discriminative features derived from the time series' properties. These features are then used to train a constrained, interpretable classifier, whose decisions can be audited and whose most important features are rigorously tested against synthetic data from known models. The outcome is not just a classifier, but a set of interpretable, statistically robust signatures that elucidate the fundamental differences between the two classes of models.

ideas.description: This research constructs a parsimonious univariate model where a single state variable (e.g., capital or output) is driven by agents' beliefs, which are updated with a one-period lag relative to an exogenous news signal. The model's parameters are disciplined by data on forecast errors and aggregate volatility. The core improvement is an adversarial validation step: the model must generate simulated data where the news shock, acting as a sunspot, produces output dynamics that pass formal statistical tests against established empirical benchmarks for belief-driven fluctuations, moving the idea from a theoretical toy to a quantitatively credible mechanism.

ideas.title: Minimal Univariate News-Sunspot Model via Ising Model Reduction
ideas.description: Drawing from statistical physics, where the complex multi-spin Ising model was reduced to a single spin in a field to demonstrate the core equivalence between microscopic interactions and macroscopic order, we construct the simplest possible univariate model to demonstrate news-sunspot equivalence in economics. The model features a single representative agent whose binary sentiment state (analogous to spin up/down) evolves in response to a composite signal. This signal is a linear combination of a fundamental news component (with exogenous noise) and a pure sunspot component (extrinsic belief-driven noise). By deriving the equilibrium condition for sentiment determination, we show mathematically that the agent's behavior depends only on the weighted sum of the two noise variances, not their separate identities. This minimal setup—requiring only one state variable—cleanly demonstrates how news and sunspots become observationally equivalent in driving economic fluctuations, mirroring how the single-spin Ising model reveals the equivalence between internal alignment forces and external magnetic fields.

ideas.title: Apply Causal Pathway Constraints from Neuroimaging to Test Model Equivalence
ideas.description: This idea transfers a model identification and comparison solution from cognitive neuroscience to the context of econometric SVAR analysis. In neuroimaging, techniques like Dynamic Causal Modeling (DCM) address the "inverse problem"—where many neural connectivity models can produce the same observed brain activity—by using anatomical and physiological priors to impose sign and pathway constraints on models, then comparing their relative plausibility. Adapted to econometrics, this approach involves formally specifying multiple competing SVAR models, each embodying a different theoretical narrative through a unique set of sign and zero restrictions on the structural matrix. Rather than using restrictions only to identify a single model, the core innovation is to treat each restricted model as a candidate and use model comparison tools (e.g., Bayesian posterior odds, comparison of impulse response fits) to test which theoretical structure is best supported, thereby directly testing the equivalence of theories that could generate similar observational time series patterns.

The core innovation is imposing a selection criterion that minimizes the informational complexity of the required news process, solving the non-uniqueness problem. The resulting mapping is defined by solving a constrained optimization problem that minimizes the entropy of the news shock distribution subject to observational equivalence. This provides a disciplined, economically interpretable bridge between the two classes of models, enabling direct comparison of their implied shock structures and empirical content.

ideas.description: This research develops a comprehensive framework to diagnose sunspot representations that explicitly accounts for the low statistical power of existing tests for non-fundamentalness. The system first applies a battery of such tests but treats their results as one imperfect input among several. It then constructs and estimates competing structural models—both fundamental and candidate non-fundamental (sunspot) representations—using identification strategies robust to MA invertibility. Finally, the framework employs formal Bayesian model comparison and out-of-sample forecasting metrics to assess which class of models receives stronger empirical support from the data, providing a conclusive diagnostic even when direct hypothesis tests are inconclusive.

ideas.title: Robust Bayesian Equivalence Testing via SVAR Identification Sets
ideas.description: This research develops a formal framework for testing model equivalence that directly confronts the identification problem in structural VARs. Instead of checking if a single candidate model satisfies a set of sign/zero restrictions, the method characterizes the entire posterior distribution of models compatible with the data and the restrictions. It then evaluates competing equivalence hypotheses by computing their posterior probabilities across this admissible set, providing a robust measure of confidence. The output is a probabilistically weighted conclusion about equivalence that is transparent about the underlying identification uncertainty.

ideas.description: This idea expands the original seed by drawing an analogy from geophysics. In seismology, a solved problem is classifying the source of seismic time series signals (e.g., earthquake vs. explosion) using machine learning techniques like CNNs that analyze waveform patterns. We adapt this solution to the context of discriminating between time series generated by news models and sunspot models. The adapted research idea involves treating the economic/solar time series as analogous to seismic signals, implementing a deep learning classifier (e.g., 1D CNN or CNN-LSTM) trained on discriminative features inspired by seismic analysis—such as spectral decompositions, phase correlations, and morphological pattern features—to automatically identify whether a given observed or simulated time series originates from a news-driven or a sunspot-driven generative model.

The outcome is a robust, behaviorally-validated measure of genuine political consensus, rather than a measure of mere rhetorical convergence.

In ecology, stability is assessed through time-series analysis of species data and network models of species interactions. The adapted solution applies NLP to media and parliamentary texts to create two parallel analyses: 1) A time-series of policy sentiment and keyword frequency, calculating statistical measures of variance and autocorrelation to gauge consensus volatility. 2) A network graph where nodes are policy concepts and political actors, with edges based on co-occurrence or semantic similarity. Network metrics like modularity (to detect ideological echo chambers), centrality (to identify pivotal concepts), and overall connectivity are then used as novel, structural indicators of the strength and fragility of the political consensus on adjustment options, providing a dynamic, systems-level view of the policy discourse.
